definition: to download a printable version of this glossary for your use.

name: Abdomen
definition: The third region of the body of a bee enclosing the honey stomach, true stomach, intestine, sting, and reproductive organs.

name: Absconding swarm
definition: An entire colony of bees that abandons their hive because of disease, wax moths, excessive heat or water, lack of resources, or other reasons.

name: Acarine disease
definition: The name of the disease caused by the tracheal mite (Acarapis woodi). See Tracheal mite.

name: Afterswarm
definition: A small swarm which may leave the hive after the first or primary swarm has departed. These after swarms usually have less bees associated with them than the primary swarm.

name: American foulbrood
definition: A brood disease of honey bees caused by the spore-forming bacterium, Paenibacillus larvae. The spore stage of the bacterium can remain viable for many years, making it difficult to eliminate the disease.

name: Apiary
definition: Colonies, hives, and other equipment assembled in one location for beekeeping operations; also known as a bee yard.

name: Apiculture
definition: The science and art of raising honey bees.

name: Apis mellifera
definition: The scientific genus and species of the Western honey bee, originating in Europe and Africa and now located around the world.

name: Paenibacillus larvae
definition: The bacterium that causes American foulbrood.

name: Bait hive -
definition: A hive or box, usually placed in an elevated location, used to attract and hopefully collect swarms.

name: Bee blower
definition: A motorized blower used as one method to remove bees from honeycombs. Typically frames are not removed from supers before using the blower.

name: Bee bread
definition: A "preserved" fermented mixture of collected pollen mixed with nectar or honey, deposited in the cells of a comb. Pollen is the primary pollen source for bees, and is used especially by the nurse bees to produce royal jelly to feed the young larvae.

name: Bee brush
definition: A small brush used to gently remove bees from combs. (Often disturbs the bees more than shaking them off the frame would.)

name: Bee escape
definition: A device used to remove bees from honey supers or buildings by permitting bees to pass one way but preventing their return.

name: Bee hive
definition: or Beehive - An artificial cavity for a bee colony to live in, usually a box or boxes with movable frames.

name: Bee louse
definition: See Braula coeca.

name: Bee metamorphosis
definition: The different stages through which a bee passes as she matures: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. During the pupal stage inside a capped cell, large nutrient reserves are used to transform both the internal and external anatomy of the bee.

name: Bee space
definition: 3/8-inch space between combs and hive parts in which bees build no comb or deposit only a small amount of propolis. Bee spaces are used as corridors to move within the hive. This is the space that the bees themselves build between and around their combs in nature, which beekeepers must respect when we design hives.

name: Beeswax
definition: A complex mixture of organic compounds secreted by four pairs of special glands on the worker bee's abdomen and used for building comb. Its melting point is from 143.6 to 147.2 degrees F.

name: Bee venom
definition: The poison secreted by special glands attached to the stinger of the female bee.

name: Boardman feeder
definition: A device for feeding bees that consists of an inverted jar of syrup with a base designed to slide into the hive entrance.

name: Bottling -
definition: Filling glass or plastic bottles or jars with honey, using either gravity to fill the jars, or a honey bottling machine for more speed and consistency.

name: Bottom board
definition: The floor of a hive that all the other hive components rest on top of. Can be a solid bottom board, or a screened bottom board, that allows hive debris to fall through onto the ground, or a waiting catch board.

name: Brace comb
definition: A small bit of stabilizing wax built between two combs or frames to fasten them together. Brace comb is also built between comb and adjacent hive materials, or between two hive parts such as top bars. See also: Burr comb.

name: Braula coeca
definition: The scientific name of a wingless fly commonly known as the bee louse. Does not feed on bees, and therefore is not considered a parasite.

name: Brood
definition: Immature bees that are still inside their cells. Brood can be in the form of eggs, larvae, or pupae of different ages.

name: Brood chamber
definition: or Brood nest - The part of the hive in which the brood is reared; may include one or more hive bodies and the combs within.

name: Burr comb
definition: A bit of wax built upon a comb or upon a wooden part in a hive but not connected to any other part. See also: Brace comb.

name: Capped brood
definition: or Sealed brood - Pupae whose cells have been sealed with a porous cover by adult workers, to isolate them during their nonfeeding pupal period.

name: Cappings
definition: A thin layer of wax used to cover full cells of honey. This layer of wax is typically sliced from the surface of a honey-filled comb so that the honey can be extracted.

name: Caste
definition: A term for social insects of the same species and sex that differ in morphology or behavior. In honey bees there are two castes, workers and queens. The drones are a different sex and therefore not a caste, though they are often miscategorized as the third caste.

name: Cell
definition: The hexagonal compartment of comb built by honey bees.

name: Chilled brood
definition: Bee larvae and pupae that have died from exposure to cold. This typically occurs in spring when the colony is expanding rapidly and on cold nights there aren't enough bees to keep the brood warm.

name: Chunk honey
definition: Honey comb cut from frames and placed in jars along with liquid honey.

name: Clarifying Tank
definition: or Clarifier - Any tank or holding vessel that is used to temporarily store honey while the wax and other material separate from the honey.

name: Cluster
definition: A large group of bees hanging together, one upon another. See also: Winter cluster.

name: Colony
definition: The queen, brood, drones, and all the worker bees living together in a single hive or other nest.

name: Comb
definition: A sheet of six-sided wax cells made by honey bees to hold brood, honey, nectar, and pollen. A sheet of comb will have a layer of cells on each side, united at their bases.

name: Comb honey
definition: Honey harvested and sold in the comb. It is produced either by cutting the comb from the frame (cut-comb honey) or when the comb is built in special frames which allow for its easy removal without cutting.

name: Creamed honey
definition: Honey which has crystallized under carefully controlled conditions to produce tiny crystals and an appealingly smooth texture. Often a starter or seed is used to help control the crystallization.

name: Crimped-wire foundation
definition: Foundation in which crimped wire is embedded vertically during the manufacturing process. The wire increases the strength of the foundation.

name: Cross-pollination
definition: See Pollination.

name: Crystallization
definition: The formation of sugar crystals in honey.

name: Cut-comb honey
definition: See Comb honey.

name: Dance language
definition: The system honey bees use to communicate the locations of food sources or potential nest sites to each other.

name: Dearth
definition: See Nectar dearth.

name: Deformed wing virus (DWV)
definition: A viral disease of honey bees, transmitted between bees and by the feeding of varroa mites.

name: Dextrose
definition: See Glucose.

name: Dividing
definition: See Splitting.

name: Division board feeder
definition: A wooden or plastic compartment that is hung in a hive replacing one or more frames and contains feed for bees.

name: Double screen
definition: A thin wooden frame with two separated layers of screen. Used to separate two colonies within the same hive, one above the other. An entrance or entrances are cut on the upper side to allow the upper colony access to the outside.

name: Drawn combs
definition: Cells that have been built out by honey bees from foundation in a frame.

name: Drift
definition: The failure of bees to return to their own hive in an apiary containing many colonies, because they have entered neighboring colonies instead.

name: Drone
definition: The male honey bee. Produced from unfertilized eggs.

name: Drone comb
definition: Comb measuring about four cells per linear inch, that bees use for drone rearing as well as honey storage.

name: Drone layer
definition: A queen with no stored sperm, who is therefore incapable of laying fertilized eggs. As a result, all young she lays will be drones.

name: Dysentery
definition: A condition of adult bees characterized by severe diarrhea that may be caused by starvation or low-quality food, confinement due to poor weather conditions, or nosema infection.

name: European foulbrood
definition: An infectious disease that only affects the brood of honey bees and is caused by the bacteria Streptococcus pluton.

name: Extracted honey
definition: Liquid honey removed from the comb.

name: Extraction
definition: The process in which honey is spun out of frames using centrifugal force with a honey extractor.

name: Extractor
definition: A machine that removes honey from the cells of comb by centrifugal force.

name: Fermentation
definition: The process of yeast utilizing sugar as a food, and as a byproduct producing alcohol. Bees remove moisture from nectar as it is ripened into honey until fermentation cannot occur.

name: Fertile queen
definition: A queen with sufficient stored sperm from drones, able to lay fertilized eggs.

name: Follower board
definition: A thin board the size of a frame that can be inserted into a hive to reduce the space available to the bees. Often done to help smaller colonies that may have trouble keeping the brood nest warm.

name: Foragers
definition: or Field bees - Worker bees, generally at least two to three weeks old, that work to collect nectar, pollen, water, and propolis for the colony.

name: Foundation
definition: A manmade thin sheet of beeswax or plastic embossed on both sides with the shape of cell bases. Used to encourage straight combs in frames, and to support the combs built on the foundation.

name: Frame
definition: A rectangular wooden or plastic framework, designed to hold comb and hang in a hive box.

name: Fructose
definition: The predominant simple sugar found in honey.

name: Fume board
definition: A rectangular cover the size of a super which has an absorbent material on the underside. A chemical is placed on the material to drive the bees out of supers for honey removal.

name: Fumagilin-B
definition: An antibiotic used in the prevention and suppression of nosema disease.

name: Glucose
definition: One of the two principal sugars found in honey; forms crystals during crystallization/granulation. Also called dextrose.

name: Grafting
definition: Removing a young worker larva from its cell and placing it in a queen cup in order to have it reared into a queen.

name: Grafting tool
definition: A probe, needle, or scoop designed for transferring larvae from worker cells to queen cells.

name: Granulation
definition: See Crystallization.

name: Hive
definition: A manmade structure used by a colony of bees as a nest cavity.

name: Hive body
definition: A box that encloses frames of comb. Often used particularly for the brood chambers to distinguish from supers, though the term can be applied to any hive box.

name: Hive stand
definition: A structure that supports the hive and keeps it elevated off the ground.

name: Hive tool
definition: A metal device used to open hives, pry and manipulate frames, and scrape wax and propolis from equipment.

name: Honey
definition: A sweet, viscous fluid produced by bees from the nectar of flowers. Composed largely of a mixture of sugars dissolved in about 17 percent water. It contains small amounts of mineral matter, vitamins, proteins, and enzymes.

name: Honeydew
definition: A sweet liquid excreted by aphids, leafhoppers, and some scale insects that is collected by bees, especially in the absence of a good source of nectar. Can be used to produce "forest honey" or "honeydew honey."

name: Honey house
definition: A building used for extracting honey and storing equipment.

name: Honey stomach
definition: or Honey crop - An expandable storage organ at the front of the honey bee’s digestive system. Used for carrying nectar, honey, or water.

name: Hopelessly queenless
definition: Used to describe a colony that has lost its queen, and also has no queen cells or young brood that can be used to raise emergency queens.

name: Increase
definition: To add to the number of colonies, usually by splitting those on hand. The phrase "making increase" is commonly used to describe growing one’s colony numbers.

name: Inner cover
definition: A lightweight cover used under a standard telescoping outer cover on a beehive.

name: Instrumental insemination
definition: The introduction of drone spermatozoa into the genital organs of a virgin queen by means of special instruments, allowing beekeeper control over the genetics of the queen's offspring.

name: Invertase
definition: An enzyme produced by the honey bee which helps to transform sucrose to glucose (dextrose) and fructose (levulose).

name: Larva
definition: (plural, larvae) - The second stage of the bee life cycle. A white, legless, grub-like insect.

name: Laying worker
definition: A worker that lays unfertilized eggs, producing only drones, usually in colonies that are hopelessly queenless.

name: Levulose
definition: See Fructose.

name: Mating flight
definition: or Nuptial flight - The flight (or flights) taken by a virgin queen while she mates in the air with multiple drones.

name: Mead
definition: Honey wine.

name: Migratory beekeeping
definition: The moving of colonies of bees from one locality to another during a single season to take advantage of multiple honey flows, and/or to fulfill agricultural pollination contracts.

name: Nectar
definition: A sweet and often fragrant liquid secreted by the nectaries of flowering plants as an attractive reward for pollinating animals. Nectar is the raw resource used to make.

name: Nectar dearth
definition: A period with little to no nectar available for foraging bees to collect.

name: Nectar flow
definition: or Honey flow - A time when nectar is plentiful and bees produce and store surplus honey. Often linked to a single particular flower, e.g. "the goldenrod flow."

name: Nectar guide
definition: Markings or patterns on flowers believed to direct insects to nectar sources.

name: Nosema disease
definition: or Nosemosis - Intestinal disease of honey bees caused by either the protozoa Nosema apis or Nosema ceranae. The microbes destroy the gut of the bee and severe infections result in malnutrition and dysentery. Symptoms of the two infections differ somewhat. (Recently reclassified as Vairimorpha apis and Vairimorpha ceranae.)

name: Nucleus
definition: or Nuc - A small colony of bees consisting of fewer frames than a typical hive, usually housed in a smaller hive box. A nuc usually consists of three to six frames of comb and is used primarily for starting new colonies or rearing or storing queens.

name: Nurse bees
definition: Young bees, usually three to ten days old, that feed and take care of developing brood.

name: Observation hive
definition: A hive made largely of glass or clear plastic to allow for the observation of bees at work.

name: Package bees
definition: A quantity of adult bees (2 to 5 pounds), usually with a queen, contained in a screened shipping cage with a food source. A package is essentially a simulated swarm.

name: PDB (Paradichlorobenzene)
definition: Crystals used to fumigate stored combs against wax moths.

name: Pheromones
definition: Chemical substances secreted from glands and used as a means of communication. Honey bees secrete many different pheromones.

name: Play flights
definition: or Orientation flights - Short flights taken in front of or near the hive to acquaint young bees with their immediate surroundings.

name: Pollen
definition: The male reproductive cells produced by anthers of flowers. Collected and used by honey bees as their source of protein.

name: Pollen basket
definition: A flattened depression surrounded by curved hairs, located on the outer surface of a worker bee's hind legs and adapted for carrying pollen to the hive.

name: Pollen substitute
definition: Any material such as soybean flour, powdered skim milk, brewer's yeast, or a mixture of these used in place of pollen as a source of protein to stimulate brood rearing. Typically fed to a hive in early spring to encourage colony expansion.

name: Pollen supplement
definition: Pollen, or a mixture of pollen and pollen substitutes, used to stimulate brood rearing as with pollen substitute.

name: Pollen trap
definition: A device designed to remove pollen loads from the pollen baskets of incoming bees.

name: Pollination
definition: The transfer of pollen from the anthers to the stigma of a flower or flowers. Honey bees are excellent pollinators.

name: Primary swarm
definition: The first swarm to leave the parent colony, usually with the old queen (see secondary swarm).

name: Propolis
definition: Plant saps and resins collected by bees and used to strengthen the comb and to seal cracks. Possesses antimicrobial and waterproofing properties.

name: Pupa
definition: (plural Pupae) - The third stage in the honey bee life cycle, during which it changes (pupates) from a larva to an adult bee.

name: Queen
definition: A female bee with a fully developed reproductive system. Larger and longer than a worker bee.

name: Queen cage
definition: A small cage in which a queen and three to five worker bee attendants are confined, for shipping and introduction into a new colony.

name: Queen cell
definition: A special elongated cell in which a queen is reared. It is an inch or longer and hangs down from the comb in a vertical orientation.

name: Queen clipping
definition: Removing a portion of one or both front wings of a queen to prevent her from flying.

name: Queen cup
definition: A shallow wax cup built by bees to allow the production of new queens. Once the queen has laid an egg in, it becomes a queen cell.

name: Queen excluder
definition: A flat sheet of metal or plastic with spaces that permit the passage of workers but restrict the movement of drones and queens, limiting their access to a certain parts of the hive.

name: Robbing
definition: Stealing of nectar or honey by bees from other colonies. More common during a nectar dearth.

name: Robbing screen
definition: or Robber screen - A device that fits over the entrance of a hive, baffling arriving robber bees while allowing the resident bees to come and go.

name: Royal jelly
definition: A highly nutritious glandular secretion of young bees, used to feed the queen and young brood.

name: Sacbrood
definition: A viral disease which affects the larvae of honey bees.

name: Scout bees
definition: Worker bees searching for a new source of pollen, nectar, propolis, water, or a new home for the swarm they are a part of.

name: Screened bottom board
definition: See Bottom board.

name: Secondary swarm
definition: A smaller swarm containing a new virgin queen which may occur after the primary swarm has departed.

name: Skep
definition: A traditional bell-shaped straw beehive without movable frames.

name: Slatted rack
definition: A wooden frame that fits between the bottom board and lowest hive body. Can reduce congestion at the hive entrance, and helps the bees control the microclimate at the bottoms of the bottom frames.

name: Slumgum
definition: The remaining refuse from melted comb and cappings after the usable wax has been rendered and removed.

name: Smoker
definition: A device in which materials are kept smoldering to produce cool smoke (not flames) to subdue bees.

name: Solar wax melter
definition: A box with a transparent lid, used to melt wax from combs and cappings by the heat of the sun.

name: Splitting
definition: Dividing a bee colony into two (or more) colonies to produce new colonies.

name: Spur embedder
definition: A handheld device used for embedding wires into wax foundation with the purpose of reinforcing the foundation.

name: Stinger
definition: or Sting - The specialized modified ovipositor of a female honey bee, used as a weapon. Worker honey bees have a barbed stinger which embeds in the elastic skin of most vertebrate victims. Queens have non-barbed stingers that are largely used to kill rival queens early in life.

name: Streptococcus pluton
definition: Bacterium that causes European foulbrood.

name: Sucrose
definition: The principal sugar found in most nectar.

name: Super
definition: Any hive body, usually a smaller box, used for the storage of honey which the beekeeper intends to harvest. Normally it is placed above the brood chamber(s). Supers are typically medium or shallow sized boxes.

name: Supersedure
definition: The natural replacement of an established mother queen by a newly reared daughter queen in the same hive.

name: Swarm
definition: A large number of worker bees, drones, and usually the old queen that leaves the parent colony to find a new home and establish a new colony.

name: Swarming
definition: The natural process through which a swarm emerges from a hive, clusters (bivouacs) on a tree branch or other surface, and then finds and moves to a new home.

name: Swarm cell
definition: Queen cells usually found on the bottom of the combs before swarming, produced to provide a new queen for the colony from which the swarm depart.

name: Terramycin
definition: An antibiotic used to control American and European foulbrood. Only available in the U.S. under the supervision of a veterinarian.

name: Tracheal mites
definition: A parasitic mite of the species Acarapis woodi. Lives in the internal trachea (breathing tubes) of adult bees.

name: Uncapping
definition: The process by which the thin layer of beeswax over capped honey is removed, allowing for honey harvesting.
cross_references: The process by which the thin layer of beeswax over capped honey is removed, allowing for honey harvesting.

name: Uncapping knife
definition: A knife used to shave or remove the cappings from combs of sealed honey prior to extraction. Some are heated by steam or electricity.

name: Uniting
definition: Combining two or more colonies to form one larger colony. Weak colonies are often combined in the fall to prepare for winter.

name: Vairimorpha apis and Vairimorpha ceranae - See Nosema disease
definition: A specialized term in apiculture representing Vairimorpha apis and Vairimorpha ceranae - See Nosema disease.
cross_references: Vairimorpha apis and Vairimorpha ceranae - See Nosema disease.

name: Varroa mite or Varroa destructor - A devastating parasitic mite of honey bees, responsible for the deaths of many colonies due to weakening the bees and transmitting multiple bee viruses
definition: A specialized term in apiculture representing Varroa mite or Varroa destructor - A devastating parasitic mite of honey bees, responsible for the deaths of many colonies due to weakening the bees and transmitting multiple bee viruses.
cross_references: Varroa mite or Varroa destructor - A devastating parasitic mite of honey bees, responsible for the deaths of many colonies due to weakening the bees and transmitting multiple bee viruses.

name: Veil - A hat, helmet, or headpiece including a screen of wire or fabric netting, used to protect the beekeeper's head and neck from stings
definition: A specialized term in apiculture representing Veil - A hat, helmet, or headpiece including a screen of wire or fabric netting, used to protect the beekeeper's head and neck from stings.
cross_references: Veil - A hat, helmet, or headpiece including a screen of wire or fabric netting, used to protect the beekeeper's head and neck from stings.

name: Virgin queen
definition: A queen that is not yet mated.

name: Waggle dance - See Dance language
definition: A specialized term in apiculture representing Waggle dance - See Dance language.

name: Wax glands
definition: Glands that secrete beeswax. Found in pairs on the underside of the last four abdominal segments of worker bees.

name: Wax moth
definition: Usually, larvae of the greater wax moth Galleria mellonella, which can seriously damage brood and empty combs.

name: Winter cluster
definition: A ball-like arrangement of adult bees within the hive during winter. Bees in winter consume honey and produce heat with their flight muscles.

name: Worker bee
definition: A female bee whose reproductive organs are undeveloped. The majority of the honey bees in a colony are worker bees, and they do all the work in the colony except for laying fertile eggs.

name: Worker comb
definition: Comb measuring about five cells to the inch, in which workers are reared and honey and pollen are stored.

definition: Customer ServiceToll-Free: 1-800-632-3379Fax: 518-314-0576

name: Business hours
definition: Monday through Friday, 9 AM - 5 PM ETSaturday, 9 AM - 1 PM ET(Our hours and planned closures)

name: Winter
definition: An open-source project for monitoring and managing beehives using sensors, machine learning, and data analytics.

name: Workload
definition: A reduction in the average daily number of tasks performed by a beekeeper, measured in units of time (e.g., hours or minutes), resulting from the implementation of precision beekeeping technologies and automation tools within their connected beehive installations.

name: Wing
definition: An open-source project for monitoring and managing beehives using sensors, machine learning, and data analytics.

name: Window
definition: A smart beehive is a self-sustaining, high-tech beehive that uses sensors, automation, and data analysis to monitor and manage the health of the bees, improve honey production, and reduce the environmental impact of beekeeping.

name: Winter Brood Rearing
definition: A non-invasive, passive bee monitoring system using computer vision and machine learning algorithms to track bee behavior, health, and population dynamics within a beehive.

name: Yolo
definition: Precision beekeeping research refers to the use of advanced technologies, including IoT sensors and machine learning algorithms, to monitor and manage bee colonies with high accuracy and efficiency.

name: Wsn
definition: A Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is a network of embedded devices with sensing, computing, and communication abilities specifically designed to gather data from a beehive, utilizing the honey bee as sensors in an interdisciplinary analysis of biological, environmental, meteorological, and engineering perspectives.

name: Zone
definition: A thermal anomaly within a beehive, characterized by an elevated temperature zone, typically observed as a region of increased heat flux on the external surface of the honeycomb, often indicative of abnormal wax excretion or high metabolic activity in worker bees.

name: Winter Period
definition: The winter period refers to the approximately 3-month duration from late October to early February, characterized by reduced bee activity, lower temperatures, and altered wax excretion patterns in worker bees, necessitating specialized monitoring techniques such as infrared imaging and acoustic reading systems.

name: Winter Season
definition: A ventilation system consisting of a network of insulated tubes and valves, designed to maintain hive temperature stability by regulating airflow and moisture levels during periods of heavy snowfall or low humidity.

name: Wood
definition: A hardwood component, typically with a smooth surface and a rectangular cross-section, used as the structural foundation of a beehive to protect the colony from moisture.

name: Wild Honeybee
definition: Apis dorsata laboriosa, a subspecies of the giant honeybee, is a large and ferocious Apid species that constructs single combs up to 20 ft tall in open areas or hanging from branches at heights of 2-100 m.

name: Wooden Box
definition: A wooden box is a hollow, rectangular container made from wood, typically with a lid and a flat bottom, used for storing or housing bee colonies, such as those of the stingless bees (Meliponinae), in apiary settings.

name: Yield
definition: The yield refers to the quantity of honey produced by a single super chamber within a Langstroth hive, measured in kilograms or pounds, and is typically calculated as the total weight of honey stored in the frames divided by the number of frames in the super chamber.

name: Wooden Log
definition: A cylindrical, naturally rot-resistant, woody structure with a diameter typically between 10-30 centimeters and a length of several meters, often harvested from deciduous or coniferous trees and used as a foundation for traditional beekeeping hives in various regions.

name: Wooden Board
definition: A flat, rectangular piece of wood with a smooth surface, typically made from a hardwood such as beech or birch, used to construct the bottom chamber of a beehive, measuring approximately 550 mm in length, 406 mm in width, and 22 mm in thickness.

name: Wooden Strip
definition: A narrow, flat strip of wood, typically made from a hardwood such as beech or birch, used to create a float in a division board feeder for feeding sugar syrup to bees.

name: Wound
definition: A decapping knife is a specially designed, heated or plain steel blade used to carefully remove the sealed portion of honeycomb cells without damaging the surrounding wax structure.

name: Yeast
definition: Yeast is a microorganism responsible for the fermentation process that converts honey's sugars into ethanol and carbon dioxide, typically killed during honey processing through indirect heating in a water bath at 60 °C for 30 minutes.

name: Wongsiri
definition: Varroa destructor is a parasitic mite species that infests the honey bee Apis cerana, causing significant harm to colony health and productivity in India.

name: Young Larvae
definition: Young larvae, specifically those between 4-5 days old, are susceptible to European Foulbrood Disease characterized by a change in color from shiny white to yellow or brown due to midgut infection.

name: Wild Plant
definition: A wild plant is a perennial or annual plant that grows naturally in the environment without human intervention, exhibiting characteristics such as seed dispersal mechanisms and chemical defenses that facilitate its survival and reproduction in the absence of agricultural management.

name: Wild Pollinator
definition: A wild pollinator is a non-domesticated, non-honey-producing bee species that visits and collects pollen from agricultural crops, facilitating pollination through its foraging behavior.

name: Wild Honey Bee
definition: Apis mellifera, a subspecies of the Western honey bee, is a domesticated eusocial bee species characterized by its distinctive waggle dance and production of honey, brood, propolis, wax, bee pollen, royal jelly, and bee venom.

name: Workplace
definition: A managed apiary, typically consisting of a cluster of hives containing Apis cerana or other native stingless bee species, maintained by a beekeeper for honey production and pollination services.

name: Winter Hardiness
definition: Winter hardiness refers to the ability of Apis mellifera mellifera colonies to survive and maintain viability during periods of prolonged cold temperatures, typically below -10°C, without significant decline in population size or honey production.

name: Young Growth
definition: Young growth refers to the newly emerged, unopened, and undeveloped shoots of Tilia cordata Mill trees, measuring between 0.5-2 meters in height, with a diameter of less than 10 millimeters, found within the mature and overgrowth stands of the forest fund of the Republic of Bashkortostan.

name: Wooden Frame
definition: A rectangular structure composed of interlocking vertical slats, typically made from a durable hardwood such as beech or birch, designed to provide a framework for suspending and supporting a sheet of comb foundation within an apiary.

name: Wine
definition: A fermented beverage produced from the enzymatic hydrolysis of starches, primarily derived from grapes or other fruits, resulting in a complex mixture of sugars, acids, and other compounds that contribute to its characteristic flavor, aroma, and color.

name: Young Bee
definition: A young bee is a newly emerged worker bee, typically within the first few weeks of its life cycle, characterized by its lack of reproductive organs and limited foraging capabilities.

name: Winter Loss
definition: Winter loss refers to the estimated 30-40% decline in honey bee colony strength and productivity during the winter months, primarily due to the cumulative effects of varroa mite infestations and inadequate cold hardiness measures.

name: Window Screen
definition: A device consisting of a mesh screen, typically made from window screen or eight-mesh hardware cloth, used to facilitate the controlled release of a queen bee into a hive during requeening operations.

name: Yard
definition: A rectangular area surrounding a hive, typically bounded by fences or hedges, designed to minimize the disturbance of neighboring residents and prevent unwanted interactions between bees and humans, animals, or other objects within a 6-foot radius.

name: Winter Feed
definition: A concentrated sugar syrup solution, typically consisting of one part granulated sugar to one part water by volume or weight, specifically formulated for supplemental feeding to honey bee colonies during periods of inadequate nectar stores and cold temperatures.

name: Young Nurse
definition: A young nurse bee is a newly emerged worker bee that has completed its first 3-4 days of development and is now responsible for feeding and caring for the brood in a honeybee colony.

name: Winter Survival
definition: Winter survival refers to the ability of a honey bee colony to maintain its population and viability through the winter months, characterized by the presence of specific treatments such as fumagillin supplementation in sugar syrup, which has been shown to significantly improve colony overwintering success rates.

name: Young Queen
definition: A young queen is a newly introduced, virgin female bee (Apis mellifera) that has been selected and prepared for mating, characterized by its vigorous egg-laying behavior and ability to establish dominance within the colony.

name: Young Brood
definition: Young brood refers to newly emerged bees, typically within the first few days of life, characterized by their soft exoskeletons and lack of fully developed wings.

name: Willingness
definition: The bees' propensity to actively consume and utilize a protein source, such as pollen or a substitute, in response to the presence of natural pollen, characterized by an increased rate of feeding and brood rearing.

name: Young Pupae
definition: Young pupae are recently sealed larvae that have not yet undergone metamorphosis, characterized by a partially formed exoskeleton and an intact sac-like structure containing the larval contents.

name: Young Adult
definition: A young adult bee is a larva that has emerged from its cell and begun to feed on wax and pollen, typically after approximately 21 days of development.

name: Wilderness
definition: A wilderness area is a region characterized by dense forest cover, rugged terrain, and limited human activity, providing suitable habitat for bears to roam and forage without direct human disturbance or fragmentation of their natural behavior patterns.

name: Wildlife
definition: A non-human, four-legged mammal (Ursidae) that intentionally damages or threatens apiary sites through aggressive behavior, such as loud noises, bright lights, or physical attacks, and is often deterred by the use of bear-deterrent fencing and baiting systems.

name: Wooden Section
definition: A wooden section is a folded, foundation-filled box with precisely calibrated dimensions and moisture-treated grooves to prevent breakage during assembly.

name: Wildflower
definition: A wildflower is a mixture of nectar and pollen collected from various, unidentified flowering plants, characterized by a lack of distinct floral source identification or certification.

name: Zhao
definition: A specific genus of parasitic mites, commonly found on honey bees (Apis mellifera), characterized by their unique ability to infest and manipulate the brood cells of Apis mellifera colonies.

name: Worldwide Apiculture
definition: Worldwide apiculture refers to the coordinated global management and monitoring of honey bee (Apis mellifera) populations, colonies, and apiary operations across diverse climatic zones, organizational structures, and geographical regions, with a focus on mitigating climate-driven impacts on pollinator health and productivity.

name: Yellow Legged Hornet
definition: Vespa velutina is a highly invasive, Asian species of hornet characterized by its distinctive yellow legs and aggressive behavior towards honeybee colonies, causing significant economic losses to the Korean beekeeping industry through its predatory foraging habits and potential transmission of diseases.

name: Zeal
definition: Zeal refers to the intentional and deliberate selection of a specific plant species, Hovenia dulcis var. koreana Nakai, as an alternative honey source in Korean apiculture due to its higher nectar secretion per flower and increased antioxidant activity compared to traditional black locust (Robinia pseudoaccaci) honey plants.

name: Write
definition: A data recording component, specifically an MQTT service, that receives telemetry push data from LoRaWAN gateways, decodes MQTT JSON payloads, and encodes them into PostgreSQL table records.

name: Workforce
definition: A seasonal workforce refers to a temporary, part-time group of beekeepers who assist migratory beekeeping operations during peak honey production periods, typically consisting of young, inexperienced beekeepers or those with limited mobility, who are hired on a short-term basis to support the primary beekeeper's workload.

name: Zander
definition: A Zander is a type of movable frame used in modern beekeeping systems, characterized by its high management potential and ability to store honey in supers, typically consisting of up to four boxes.

name: Young People
definition: Young people: individuals between the ages of 15 and 30 who have completed secondary education and are actively involved in beekeeping, characterized by a high demand for modern beehives due to their increased management potential and honey yields compared to traditional hives.

name: Workshop
definition: A specialized, hands-on educational session for beekeepers, typically involving practical demonstrations and training on specific techniques or equipment related to apiculture.

name: Zhejiang Province
definition: Zhejiang Province refers to the region where Chinese beekeepers initiate early-season build-up of honeybee colonies as early as the beginning of the year, with a focus on maintaining combs balanced to bees and adjusting frame additions to optimize colony health and productivity in response to temperature fluctuations and seasonal blooming patterns.

name: Yearly Basis
definition: A yearly basis refers to the annual cycle of migratory beekeeping, wherein apiaries are relocated between northern and southern regions to coincide with seasonal honey flows, typically involving long-distance transportation via trucks or other vehicles exceeding 3000 km in a single year.

name: Youth
definition: A young bee, typically within the first two years of its life cycle, characterized by a specific caste structure and social hierarchy within the colony, exhibiting rapid growth and development under optimal environmental conditions.

name: Woodland
definition: A woodland refers to a forest ecosystem characterized by a dense canopy, typically with an understory of shrubs and herbaceous vegetation, providing a habitat for a diverse array of flora and fauna, including the Himalayan honey bee (Apis laboriosa) and other indigenous Apis species.

name: Wild Plant Diversity
definition: Wild plant diversity refers to the complex network of relationships between flowering plants, their pollinators, and other organisms that facilitate the reproduction and dispersal of plant species in natural ecosystems.

name: Wintering Building
definition: A specially designed, climate-controlled structure with a minimum internal temperature threshold of 32°C and maximum relative humidity level of 80%, specifically constructed to house beehives during periods of extreme cold weather.

name: Yellow Honey
definition: Yellow honey is a viscous, golden-colored liquid produced by Apis mellifera colonies in the Bench-Maji Zone during November and December harvest periods, characterized by a distinct floral aroma and a pH level of 3.8-4.2.

name: Wisdom
definition: Wisdom refers to the informed, context-dependent decision-making capacity of a beekeeper to navigate complex socio-ecological environments, integrating scientific data, stakeholder perspectives, and local knowledge to achieve sustainable outcomes for their apiary operations.

name: Zero Crossing
definition: Zero crossing refers to the number of times the instantaneous amplitude of a honey bee sound signal crosses zero within a single oscillation, as defined by Intrinsic Mode Function (IMF) properties A and B.

name: Window Size
definition: A window size refers to a fixed, integer value representing the number of contiguous audio samples used as input for spectral analysis and feature extraction in audio processing applications.

name: Wsn Node
definition: A WSN node is a specialized, low-power computing device designed to collect and transmit data from various sensors within a beehive environment, utilizing an ATmega1281 microcontroller and implementing energy harvesting and sleep modes for extended lifetime operation.

name: Zigbee Network
definition: A Zigbee network is a low-power, wireless communication protocol used to synchronize and transmit data between end devices, such as sensor nodes, and a base station in an IoT-enabled beehive system, where data is collected and transmitted via a 3G radio to a server for cloud storage.

name: Worrying Situation
definition: A non-random sampling method involving a multi-annual approach or questionnaire that implies potential bias and differs from traditional surveys, characterized by its orientation-based design and limited spatial and temporal comparisons.

name: Winter Colony
definition: A winter colony is a group of honey bees (Apis mellifera) that survive the winter season by clustering together within their hive, relying on stored honey and pollen for sustenance, and typically exhibiting reduced population sizes compared to summer colonies.

name: Writing
definition: A process of recording the sequential arrangement of wax scales on a honeycomb cell, typically performed by bees using their mandibles to deposit and shape the wax in a precise, geometric pattern.

name: Winter Loss Rate
definition: The percentage of honey bee colonies that die during the winter season, calculated as the ratio of total winter colony losses to the initial number of colonies at the start of the winter period.

name: Winter Food
definition: A concentrated, high-energy food source consisting of a mixture of sugars and water, specifically designed to sustain honey bee colonies during periods of extreme cold and reduced forage availability, typically harvested from the nectar of certain winter-flowering plant species such as coniferous trees or winter heather.

name: Winter Mortality
definition: Winter mortality refers to the percentage decrease in honey bee colony survival rates during the winter months, specifically measured as a decline in the number of colonies that survive with a minimum viable population threshold (>10,000 bees) compared to the previous year's average.

name: Wonder
definition: A phenomenon characterized by an intense, awe-inspired response to the intricate social hierarchy and complex communication patterns of Apis mellifera colonies, often accompanied by a heightened sense of appreciation for the role of these organisms in maintaining ecosystem balance and biodiversity.

name: Zippered Veil
definition: A zippered veil is a fabric component attached to the upper portion of a beekeeping suit, specifically designed to protect the face and neck from bee stings during hive inspections.

name: Working Condition
definition: A well-maintained, precision-crafted, handheld smoker with a fuel source of dry, seasoned wood or alternative combustible materials, designed to calm bees during hive inspections and maintenance activities by producing a consistent, smoke-rich vapor.

name: Winter Insulation
definition: A layer of 2-3 cm thick, breathable insulation material (e.g., straw or wood shavings) placed around the hive entrance to reduce heat loss and prevent moisture accumulation during cold winter months.

name: Wound Healing
definition: Topical application of aloe vera gel, containing 0.5-2% anthraquinone compounds, facilitates wound healing by enhancing collagen synthesis and modulating the activity of matrix metalloproteinases in Apis mellifera honeybee-derived topical creams.

name: Zigbee Radio
definition: A Zigbee radio is a low-power, wireless communication module designed for mesh networking and device-to-device communication within a specific frequency band (typically 2.4 GHz), characterized by its ability to form networks of interconnected devices that can dynamically adjust their transmission power and data rate in response to changes in the network environment.

name: Windiness
definition: Windiness refers to the sustained gusts exceeding 5 m s−1 that disrupt the aerodynamic stability of a hive, thereby reducing honey production and necessitating adjustments in ventilation and temperature management within the apiary.

name: Zootherapy
definition: Zootherapy refers to the therapeutic application of animal-derived products, such as venom, blood, or meat, to treat human diseases and physical pathologies, including apitherapy using honey bee products.

name: Wild Insect Pollinator
definition: A wild insect pollinator is a non-domesticated, free-flying insect species that facilitates the transfer of pollen between plants, including native and invasive species, in urban and natural environments.

name: Wild Pollinator Conservation
definition: Wild pollinator conservation refers to the systematic monitoring and management of urban ecosystems to maintain ecological interaction networks, mitigate colony decline, and promote regional species pool utilization through targeted habitat enrichment and policy interventions regulating beekeeper density and hive placement.

name: Wool
definition: A natural fiber obtained from the hair of sheep, goats, or other animals, characterized by its crimped texture and ability to be spun into yarn for textile production.

name: Woman
definition: A female beekeeper is a person who constructs and maintains beehives, typically wearing protective clothing to prevent stings, and is often depicted in medieval manuscripts as assisting in the harvesting of honeycomb from skeps placed on shelves or in apiaries.

name: Zinc
definition: Zinc is a naturally occurring, bluish-white metalloid element with the atomic number 30, characterized by its high density and ability to inhibit bacterial growth in honey production environments.

name: Willow
definition: Salix, a deciduous tree genus native to the Northern Hemisphere, characterized by slender branches and catkins that produce allergenic pollen grains, often collected by bees for honey production.

name: Woreda
definition: A woreda is a rural administrative division in Ethiopia, typically consisting of approximately 200-300 households and serving as the primary unit for agricultural extension services, including modern beekeeping initiatives.

name: Winter Time
definition: A period characterized by a significant decrease in ambient temperature, typically between December and February in the Northern Hemisphere, marked by reduced bee activity, slower honey flow rates, and altered pheromone profiles.

name: Wildfire
definition: A wildfire is a rapidly spreading, uncontrolled fire that consumes vegetation and other combustible materials, releasing large amounts of particulate matter into the air, which can be detected by gas sensor arrays used to monitor environmental contaminants in bee hives.

name: Winter Treatment
definition: A winter treatment refers to a targeted application of a controlled-release or slow-acting substance, typically a mixture of organic acids and/or veterinary medicinal products, applied to the apiary in December, between Christmas and New Year, with the primary goal of preventing Varroa mite infestations and promoting colony health during the dormant season.

name: Worship
definition: Functional foods derived from bee products, including honey, pollen, bee bread, royal jelly, and propolis, offering nutritional and health benefits.

name: Wing Deformity
definition: A wing deformity refers to a morphological anomaly characterized by an abnormal shape, size, or structure of the wings in Apis mellifera, resulting from damage caused by Tropilaelaps mercedesae infestation, which shortens the life span of the host and reduces emergence weight.

name: Yearly Honey Production
definition: Yearly honey production refers to the total quantity of raw, unfiltered honey harvested from Korean bee colonies within a 12-month period, measured in tons, and calculated based on factors such as weather conditions, floral availability, and beekeeping practices.

name: Abscond
definition: When honey bees abscond, it means that they have abandoned their hive and left it completely empty. This can be a devastating event for beekeepers, as it usually means the loss of an entire colony of bees. Honey bees may abscond for a variety of reasons, including disease, pests, lack of food or water, or environmental stressors. In some cases, the bees may simply outgrow their hive and decide to move to a larger location. Whatever the reason, absconding is a natural behavior for bees, and it is not necessarily a sign of poor hive management or neglect. However, it is important for beekeepers to monitor their hives regularly and take steps to prevent absconding whenever possible, such as providing adequate food and water, maintaining a healthy hive environment, and addressing any potential sources of stress or disease. Absconding is not the same as swarming.

name: Acute Paralysis Virus
definition: Honey bee Acute Paralysis Virus (APV) is a highly contagious and devastating disease that affects honey bees of all ages. APV is transmitted through direct contact between bees or through contact with contaminated equipment or food sources. Once infected, bees can experience a range of symptoms, including tremors, paralysis, and death. APV is particularly concerning to beekeepers because it can spread rapidly through a hive, leading to the loss of entire colonies. While there is no cure for APV, beekeepers can take steps to prevent its spread, such as regularly monitoring their hives for signs of infection, quarantining infected bees, and maintaining good hygiene practices. By staying vigilant and taking proactive measures, beekeepers can help protect their bees from the devastating effects of APV and other diseases.

name: Adulterated Honey
definition: Adulterated honey is a type of honey that has been processed or modified in some way to increase its volume, alter its flavor, or reduce its cost. Adulteration can occur in many ways, including mixing honey with other sweeteners such as corn syrup or molasses, diluting honey with water, or exposing honey to high heat or pressure. The finished product is then mislabeled as Pure Honey without acknowledging the additives to the consumers, which is also known as food fraud. Adulterated honey can be difficult to detect, as it may look and taste similar to pure honey. However, there are certain indicators that can help consumers and beekeepers identify adulterated honey, such as a lack of pollen or the presence of additives or fillers. The proliferation of adulterated honey is a growing concern for the beekeeping industry, as it can lead to decreased demand for pure honey and lower prices for beekeepers. In addition, adulterated honey can be harmful to consumers, as it may contain contaminants or allergens that are not present in pure honey. To combat the problem of adulterated honey, it is important for consumers to purchase honey from reputable sources and for beekeepers to take steps to ensure the purity and quality of their honey, such as regular testing and monitoring. By working together, we can help protect the integrity of this valuable and delicious natural resource.

name: Africanized bee
definition: a population of bees in the Americas, also called “killer” bees, which has resulted from importation of bees into Brazil from Africa in the mid-1950s known for their defensiveness

name: Africanized Honey Bees
definition: Africanized bees, also known as "killer bees," are a hybrid species that was created in the 1950s when African honey bees were introduced to Brazil in an attempt to boost honey production. These bees quickly interbred with the local honey bee population, resulting in a new species with a more aggressive temperament and greater ability to defend their hives. Africanized bees have since spread throughout South and Central America and into the southern United States, where they are now considered an invasive species. These bees are known for their highly defensive behavior, which can be triggered by even minor disturbances, such as loud noises or vibrations. When threatened, Africanized bees will swarm and attack in large numbers, chasing their target for up to a quarter mile or more. This behavior can be particularly dangerous for humans and animals, as even a single sting can cause severe allergic reactions or death in some cases. In addition to their aggressive behavior, Africanized bees are also known for their tendency to colonize in unusual locations, such as inside walls, attics, and other enclosed spaces. This can make it difficult for beekeepers and pest control professionals to safely remove these colonies without causing harm to the bees or nearby people. Despite their reputation as "killer bees," it is important to remember that Africanized bees are still a valuable and important part of our ecosystem. They play a critical role in pollinating plants and crops, and they produce honey that is similar in taste and quality to that of traditional honey bees. However, it is important to respect and understand the behavior of Africanized bees and take appropriate safety precautions when interacting with them.

name: Alarm Pheromone
definition: The honey bee alarm pheromone is a powerful chemical signal that is used by bees to alert other members of the colony to potential threats or danger. When a honey bee detects a potential threat, such as a predator or intruder, it will release the alarm pheromone from a gland located near its stinger. This pheromone is made up of a complex mixture of chemicals, including isopentyl acetate and 2-heptanone, which are highly volatile and easily detected by other bees in the colony. When other bees detect the alarm pheromone, they will immediately become more agitated and defensive, preparing to defend the hive against the perceived threat. This behavior can include increased buzzing, more aggressive posturing, and a greater willingness to sting. The alarm pheromone can also be used to mark a location, such as a food source, that is under attack by predators or other bees. This allows other bees to avoid the area or take appropriate defensive measures to protect the valuable resource. While the alarm pheromone is a critical tool for honey bees in defending their hives and resources, it can also be a nuisance for beekeepers and humans who come into contact with it. The pheromone can be released in response to a wide range of stimuli, including unexpected movements, vibrations, and even certain scents. This can lead to bees becoming agitated and defensive, posing a risk to humans and animals in the area. To prevent the release of the alarm pheromone, beekeepers and others should take care to avoid disturbing bees unnecessarily and to move slowly and calmly when working near hives. By respecting the behavior of honey bees and taking appropriate safety precautions, we can help minimize the risk of harm to both humans and bees.

name: Alcohol Wash
definition: The honey bee alcohol wash is a method used by beekeepers to test their hives for the presence of varroa mites, a parasitic mite that can cause significant damage to honey bee colonies. This method involves collecting a sample of bees from the hive and washing them in a solution of rubbing alcohol and water. To perform an alcohol wash, beekeepers will typically collect anywhere from 300-500 bees from the brood chamber of the hive. The bees are then placed in a jar or container and covered with a solution of 70-80% rubbing alcohol and water. The container is then shaken vigorously for several minutes, causing the mites to release from the bees and float down to the bottom. The mites can then be counted to determine the level of infestation in the hive. While the alcohol wash can be an effective method for testing hives for varroa mites, it is also lethal to the bees. Collecting a large number of bees from the hive can disrupt the colony's normal behavior and cause stress. To minimize the risk of harm to the bees, beekeepers should use caution when performing an alcohol wash and follow strict safety guidelines. By taking appropriate measures to control varroa mite infestations, beekeepers can help ensure the health and longevity of their colonies. If you do decide to do an alcohol wash, please make sure you know where your queen is BEFORE scooping up the bees; all too often beekeepers accidentally scoop up the queen with the sample bees, leaving their hive queenless.

name: Alighting Board
definition: A bee hive entrance alighting landing board is a platform located at the entrance of a honey bee hive that serves as a landing zone for incoming bees. This board provides a stable surface for bees to land on as they return to the hive, allowing them to rest and reorient themselves before entering the hive. The alighting landing board is typically made of wood or plastic and is attached to the front of the hive, extending outward from the entrance. The design of the landing board is important for ensuring the safety and efficiency of the hive. The board should be wide enough to provide ample space for bees to land and maneuver, and it should be positioned at a slight upward angle to prevent water from pooling on the surface. Some landing boards may also include additional features, such as a groove or notch that allows bees to more easily access the entrance of the hive. In addition to providing a landing zone for bees, the alighting landing board can also serve as a useful tool for beekeepers. By observing the activity and behavior of bees on the board, beekeepers can gain important insights into the health and status of the hive. For example, a high number of dead or injured bees on the board may indicate a problem with the hive, such as disease or pest infestation. Similarly, an excessive buildup of debris or waste on the board may indicate that the hive needs to be cleaned or ventilated. By regularly monitoring the alighting landing board, beekeepers can help ensure the health and productivity of their hives.

name: Allergic Reaction
definition: An allergic reaction to a bee sting occurs when the immune system overreacts to the venom injected by a bee, resulting in a range of symptoms that can range from mild to severe. In some cases, an allergic reaction can be life-threatening and requires immediate medical attention. Symptoms of an allergic reaction to a bee sting can include swelling, itching, redness, and pain at the site of the sting, as well as hives, nausea, vomiting, and difficulty breathing. In severe cases, the individual may experience anaphylaxis, a potentially life-threatening reaction that can cause swelling of the throat and tongue, difficulty breathing, rapid heartbeat, and loss of consciousness. Individuals who have previously experienced an allergic reaction to a bee sting are at greater risk of developing a more severe reaction with subsequent stings. It is important for individuals who are allergic to bee stings to carry an epinephrine auto-injector, such as an EpiPen, with them at all times and to seek immediate medical attention if they are stung. In addition, individuals who are allergic to bee stings should take precautions to avoid being stung, such as wearing protective clothing and avoiding areas where bees are known to be present. By being aware of the signs and symptoms of an allergic reaction to a bee sting and taking appropriate safety measures, individuals can help minimize the risk of harm and ensure their safety.

name: American foulbrood (AFB)
definition: a brood disease of honey bees caused by the spore-forming bacterium Paenibacillus (formerly Bacillus) larvae.

name: Amino B Booster
definition: Amino B Booster is a supplement designed to support and enhance the health and productivity of honey bee colonies. This supplement is made from a blend of naturally occurring amino acids, vitamins, minerals, and other essential nutrients that are known to be beneficial for bees. Amino B Booster is typically added to sugar syrup or pollen substitutes and fed to bees during times of high stress or low nutritional availability, such as during the winter months or periods of drought or poor forage. The amino acids in Amino B Booster are particularly important for bees, as they are essential building blocks for the proteins and enzymes that are necessary for the bees' growth and development. In addition, the vitamins and minerals in Amino B Booster can help support the bees' immune systems, helping to prevent disease and infection. The use of Amino Bee Booster has been shown to improve the overall health and productivity of honey bee colonies, leading to increased brood production, stronger immune systems, and greater honey yields. In addition, Amino Bee Booster has been shown to be safe and effective when used according to the manufacturer's instructions. By providing honey bees with the essential nutrients they need to thrive, Amino Bee Booster can help beekeepers ensure the health and productivity of their hives, while also promoting the overall health and wellbeing of the honey bee population.

name: Anaphylactic shock
definition: constriction of the muscles surrounding the bronchial tubes of a human, which can be caused by hypersensitivity to venom and result in sudden death unless immediate medical attention is received

name: Antenna
definition: The antennae of honey bees are a critical part of their anatomy, serving a wide range of important functions that are essential to the bees' survival and behavior. Honey bee antennae are located on the top of the bee's head and are made up of several segments that are connected by flexible joints. The length and shape of the antenna segments can vary between bees, with worker bees typically having shorter antennae than queen bees or drones. One of the primary functions of honey bee antennae is to detect and interpret chemical signals, such as pheromones, that are produced by other bees in the hive. The antennae are covered in thousands of tiny hairs that are highly sensitive to these chemical signals, allowing bees to communicate with each other and coordinate their behavior. In addition to chemical signals, honey bee antennae can also detect other sensory information, such as temperature, humidity, and air movement. Honey bee antennae also play a critical role in navigation and orientation. Bees use their antennae to sense changes in the direction and intensity of the Earth's magnetic field, allowing them to navigate to and from the hive with remarkable accuracy. The antennae can also sense vibrations and other physical cues that help bees locate food sources and communicate with other members of the colony. The biggest function of the queen's antenna is to measure the size of the cell that she is about to lay an egg in; if it's a smaller cell, she knows to lay a fertilized worker egg, but if it's a bigger cell, she knows to lay an unfertilized drone egg. Overall, honey bee antennae are a remarkable and complex part of the bee's anatomy, serving a wide range of important functions that are essential to the bees' survival and behavior. By understanding the role and function of honey bee antennae, beekeepers and researchers can gain a deeper understanding of the behavior and biology of these fascinating insects.

name: Anther
definition: The anther is a critical part of the flower, serving a key role in the process of pollination and the production of seeds. The anther is located at the top of the flower's stamen and is responsible for producing and releasing pollen, which contains the male reproductive cells of the plant. The structure of the anther can vary between different types of plants, but it typically consists of two lobes that are connected by a filament. Each lobe contains several pollen sacs, which are filled with thousands of tiny grains of pollen. As the flower matures, the anther will typically open up, exposing the pollen sacs and allowing the pollen to be released into the environment. The release of pollen from the anther is a key step in the process of pollination. When a bee or other pollinator visits the flower, the pollen grains will stick to its body and be transported to another flower of the same species, where they can fertilize the female reproductive cells and produce seeds. Overall, the anther is a critical part of the flower's reproductive system, serving a key role in the process of pollination and the production of seeds. By understanding the structure and function of the anther, we can gain a deeper appreciation for the remarkable and complex biology of plants.

name: Apiarist
definition: An apiarist (Beekeeper) is a person who specializes in the care, management, and study of honey bees. Also known as a beekeeper, an apiarist is responsible for maintaining healthy and productive honey bee colonies, harvesting honey and other bee products, and ensuring the overall well-being of the bees under their care. The work of an apiarist can vary depending on their specific area of focus and the size and scope of their operation. Some apiarists may work with just a few hives as a hobby or side business, while others may manage hundreds or even thousands of hives for commercial production. Regardless of the scale of their operation, all apiarists share a deep passion and respect for the bees under their care. The work of an apiarist typically involves a wide range of tasks, including inspecting hives for signs of disease or infestation, managing honey production and harvest, and providing bees with food and water during times of low forage. Apiarists must also be skilled at handling bees safely and effectively, using specialized equipment such as protective clothing, smokers, and hive tools. In addition to managing hives, many apiarists also conduct research and study on honey bees and their behavior. This can include studying the biology and genetics of honey bees, developing new techniques for hive management and disease prevention, and exploring the role of honey bees in the larger ecosystem. Overall, the work of an apiarist is complex and challenging, requiring a deep knowledge of honey bee biology and behavior, as well as a strong commitment to the health and well-being of the bees under their care. Through their work, apiarists play a critical role in supporting the health and productivity of honey bee populations and the broader ecosystem as a whole.

name: Apiguard
definition: Apiguard is a handy treatment that helps beekeepers tackle the pesky Varroa destructor mites, which can wreak havoc on honey bee colonies. It contains thymol, a natural substance extracted from thyme, and works by releasing vapors that are effective against the mites while being safe for the bees and their honey. When beekeepers place Apiguard in the hive, it gradually diffuses into the air, allowing the mites to absorb it and helping to reduce their population. The bees, in turn, actively remove the thymol gel from the tray and distribute it throughout the hive as they clean, effectively spreading the treatment without harming the bees themselves. By incorporating Apiguard into regular hive maintenance, beekeepers can significantly improve the health and resilience of their bees, ensuring that the colonies thrive and continue to produce honey.

name: Apiology
definition: Apiology is the scientific study of honey bees and other bees, including their behavior, biology, and ecology. Apiology is a multidisciplinary field that combines aspects of biology, ecology, entomology, and agriculture, among others. The study of honey bees and other bees is important for a variety of reasons, including their role as pollinators and their significance in the production of honey, wax, and other products. Apiologists study a wide range of topics related to bees, including their anatomy, physiology, and genetics, as well as their interactions with other species and their environment. This includes studying the behavior of individual bees, such as foraging and communication, as well as the behavior of colonies as a whole. Apiologists also study the role of bees in pollination and the ecology of insect communities, including the interactions between bees and other pollinators. One of the key goals of apiology is to develop sustainable and responsible practices for beekeeping and pollination. This involves developing strategies for managing honey bee colonies and maintaining healthy populations of bees, as well as identifying and addressing threats to bee populations, such as disease, pests, and habitat loss. Apiology is an important field of study that plays a critical role in understanding and preserving the health and productivity of bee populations. By studying bees and their behavior, apiologists can help develop strategies for sustainable beekeeping and pollination, ensuring the availability of honey and other bee products for generations to come.

name: Apiphobia
definition: Apiphobia is a term used to describe the extreme fear or phobia of bees and other stinging insects. For individuals with apiphobia, the fear of bees can be so intense that it interferes with their daily lives and activities. Symptoms of apiphobia can include panic attacks, sweating, rapid heartbeat, and avoidance behaviors, such as refusing to go outside or engaging in activities that may bring them into contact with bees. Apiphobia can be caused by a variety of factors, including past traumatic experiences with bees, learned behaviors from family or peers, or a genetic predisposition to anxiety disorders. While the fear of bees is common and understandable, apiphobia can have serious consequences for individuals and their families, impacting their ability to enjoy outdoor activities or participate in social events. Fortunately, there are effective treatments for apiphobia, including cognitive-behavioral therapy and exposure therapy, which can help individuals overcome their fear of bees and other stinging insects. By working with a qualified mental health professional, individuals with apiphobia can learn strategies for managing their anxiety and gradually confront their fear of bees in a safe and controlled environment. With proper treatment, individuals with apiphobia can overcome their fear and regain their ability to enjoy the outdoors and participate in activities without fear of bees.

name: Apitherapy
definition: Apitherapy is a complementary therapy that uses bee products, such as honey, pollen, propolis, royal jelly, and bee venom, for the treatment of various health conditions. This therapy has been used for thousands of years in many cultures around the world, and is believed to have a range of health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. Apitherapy can be administered in a variety of ways, depending on the specific bee product being used and the health condition being treated. For example, honey can be consumed orally or used topically for wound healing, while bee venom is typically administered through bee stings or injections for the treatment of conditions such as arthritis and multiple sclerosis. While the use of bee products for medicinal purposes has been practiced for centuries, the scientific evidence supporting the efficacy of apitherapy is still limited. As such, it is important for individuals who are interested in trying apitherapy to consult with a qualified healthcare provider and to be aware of the potential risks and side effects of these treatments. Overall, apitherapy is a fascinating and potentially beneficial therapy that has been used for thousands of years. While further research is needed to fully understand the benefits and risks of bee products for medicinal purposes, the use of these natural substances holds promise for the treatment of a wide range of health conditions.

name: Attendants
definition: Attendants are a special group of worker bees that are responsible for caring for the queen honey bee and meeting her every need. These attendants are handpicked from the pool of worker bees in the colony and undergo specialized training to prepare them for their role. Queen attendants are responsible for feeding and grooming the queen, as well as regulating her temperature and ensuring that she is protected from harm. They will also follow the queen wherever she goes, creating a protective barrier around her and fanning their wings to circulate air and keep her cool. In addition to their physical care of the queen, attendants also play a key role in the social dynamics of the hive. They will often engage in "piping" behavior, emitting a high-pitched sound that signals to the queen and other bees in the hive. This behavior can be used to communicate a range of messages, from signaling the presence of a new queen to coordinating the activities of the hive. Overall, honey bee queen attendants are critical to the health and success of the hive. Their specialized care of the queen ensures that she is healthy and able to produce a strong and healthy brood, while their social behavior helps to maintain the complex and highly organized structure of the hive. By working together with the queen and other members of the colony, queen attendants help ensure the survival of the hive and the honey bee population as a whole.

name: Automatic uncapper
definition: automated device that removes the cappings from honey combs, usually by moving the frames between heated knives, metal teeth, or flails

name: Bacillus larvae
definition: former name of the bacterium that causes American foulbrood

name: Backfilling
definition: Honey bee backfilling is a natural process that occurs within a bee colony when the bees fill empty cells in the brood nest with nectar instead of eggs or young larvae. This can happen for a variety of reasons, such as a sudden increase in nectar flow or a decrease in the number of adult bees available to care for the brood. Backfilling can have both positive and negative impacts on the hive. On the one hand, it can help increase the amount of honey stores available for the bees to survive on during lean times. On the other hand, it can reduce the amount of space available for the queen to lay eggs, potentially leading to the bees preparing to swarm. Beekeepers need to be aware of the signs of backfilling and take appropriate measures to manage the hive and prevent potential issues. This may include adding additional supers or frames to the hive, providing supplemental feeding, or requeening the colony. By understanding the process of honey bee backfilling, beekeepers can better manage their hives and ensure the health and productivity of their colonies.

name: Baggie Feeder
definition: A honey bee baggie feeder is a simple and effective tool used by beekeepers to provide supplemental feeding to their colonies. The feeder consists of a small plastic baggie filled with sugar syrup or other liquid food and placed directly on top of the frames in the hive. The bees are able to access the syrup through small slits or holes in the baggie, allowing them to easily and efficiently collect the food they need to thrive. Honey bee baggie feeders are especially useful during times of low nectar flow or when colonies are establishing themselves, as they can help ensure that the bees have enough food to build up their population and produce honey. They are also a convenient and low-maintenance option for beekeepers, as they require minimal setup and can be easily replaced when empty. However, it's important to monitor the feeder regularly and ensure that the baggies are not leaking or attracting pests such as ants or wasps. By using a honey bee baggie feeder, beekeepers can provide their colonies with the nourishment they need to thrive.

name: Bait hive
definition: A honey bee bait hive is a specially designed hive used by beekeepers to attract and capture swarms of bees. The bait hive is typically a smaller, temporary hive that is placed in a location where bee swarms are likely to occur, such as a tree or open field. Bait hives are usually baited with a scent of beeswax, honey, or queen pheromones, which mimic the scent of a thriving bee colony. When scout bees are looking for a new home, they are attracted to the scent of the bait hive and will investigate it as a potential new colony site. The beekeeper can then capture the swarm once they move in, and transfer it to a permanent hive. Honey bee bait hives are a useful tool for beekeepers to capture and expand their colonies, as well as for conserving wild bee populations. They are also an effective and environmentally friendly alternative to using pesticides or other methods to control swarms. By using a honey bee bait hive, beekeepers can harness the natural instincts of bees and create a thriving and sustainable beekeeping operation.

name: Balling
definition: Honey bee queen balling is a natural defensive behavior exhibited by worker bees when they perceive a threat to the safety of their colony. Queen balling occurs when worker bees surround and enclose the queen bee in a tight ball, effectively trapping her and preventing her from moving or laying eggs. This behavior is usually triggered by the presence of a rival queen or an intruder, such as a wasp or hornet, that poses a threat to the colony. Queen balling is a complex and coordinated behavior that involves the release of pheromones and the use of body heat to raise the temperature of the ball, which can reach up to 113 degrees Fahrenheit. While queen balling can be effective in protecting the colony from external threats, it can also be harmful to the queen bee, who may be injured or killed in the process.
