Beekeepers need to be aware of the signs of queen balling and take appropriate measures to manage their hives and prevent potential issues. This may include removing any rival queens or intruders, providing supplemental feeding to the colony, or requeening the hive. By understanding the process of honey bee queen balling, beekeepers can better manage their hives and ensure the health and productivity of their colonies.

name: Banking Queens
definition: Banking queens is a beekeeping practice used to preserve and maintain a queen bee for future use. This involves removing a healthy queen from a colony and placing her in a special container or "bank," where she is kept alive and fed until she is needed to establish a new colony or replace a failing queen. Banking queens is a valuable technique for beekeepers, as it allows them to maintain a supply of healthy and genetically diverse queens without relying on outside sources. When a beekeeper needs a new queen, they can simply retrieve one from their queen bank and introduce her to the colony. This can save time and money, as well as help ensure the health and productivity of the colony. Banking queens requires careful monitoring and management, as the queens need to be kept in optimal conditions and fed a specialized diet to remain healthy. By using this technique, beekeepers can take an active role in preserving and maintaining the health of their colonies, while also contributing to the preservation of the honey bee population as a whole.

name: Bearding
definition: Honey bee bearding is a natural behavior exhibited by bees when the temperature inside the hive becomes too warm for them to regulate their body temperature. This occurs when the number of bees in the hive is too high, and the air circulation is poor. Bearding occurs when bees gather in large clusters on the outside of the hive, forming a beard-like appearance. This allows the bees to cool down and regulate their body temperature by fanning their wings and creating air movement. Bearding is a sign that the colony is healthy and thriving, as it indicates a large population of bees and good brood rearing. However, it can also be a sign that the hive is overcrowded, and the beekeeper may need to add additional space or frames to accommodate the growing colony. Beekeepers need to be aware of the signs of bearding and take appropriate measures to manage their hives and prevent potential issues. This may include adding additional ventilation or providing supplemental feeding to the colony. By understanding the process of honey bee bearding, beekeepers can better manage their hives and ensure the health and productivity of their colonies.

name: Bee Dance
definition: The honey bee dance is a unique and fascinating behavior that honey bees use to communicate the location of food sources to other members of the hive. When a bee discovers a new source of nectar or pollen, it returns to the hive and performs a series of intricate movements that convey information about the location, distance, and quality of the food source. The honey bee dance consists of two main components: the round dance and the waggle dance. The round dance is used to communicate the location of nearby food sources, within about 50 meters of the hive. The bee performs a circular dance, moving in a tight circle while rapidly vibrating its abdomen. Other bees in the hive can detect the vibration and follow the movements of the dancer to locate the food source. The waggle dance, on the other hand, is used to communicate the location of more distant food sources, up to several kilometers away from the hive. The bee performs a figure-eight pattern, waggling its abdomen back and forth while moving in a straight line. The angle and duration of the waggle dance convey information about the direction and distance of the food source, while the intensity of the vibrations indicates the quality of the food. Overall, the honey bee dance is a remarkable example of how honey bees use complex behaviors to communicate and coordinate their activities within the hive. By understanding the intricacies of the dance, beekeepers and researchers can gain valuable insights into the behavior and biology of these remarkable insects.

name: Bee Dun
definition: During honey extraction time, honey bee removal aids such as Bee Dun are used to help separate the bees from the honeycombs, allowing beekeepers to extract honey from the combs without harming the bees or damaging the hive. Bee Dun is sprayed onto the underside of the fume board, which is then in turn placed on top of the hive so that the metal top of the fume board can heat up in the sun, which activates the Bee Dun. The non-chemical product emits a smell that the bees don't like, so they move down out of the honey super, allowing the beekeeper to remove it from the hive with little to no bees inside. Overall, honey bee removal aids are an important tool for beekeepers during honey extraction time. By using these specialized tools and equipment, beekeepers can safely and efficiently remove honey from the hive without harming the bees or damaging the hive, ensuring that both the honey and the bees are protected.

name: Bee Go
definition: Bee Go is a natural, non-toxic bee repellent that is used by beekeepers and other professionals to safely and effectively move bees from one location to another. It works by using a blend of natural oils and scents that are unpleasant to bees, encouraging them to move away from the treated area without causing them harm. One of the main benefits of Bee Go is that it is a safe and environmentally friendly alternative to more traditional bee removal methods, such as pesticides or smoke. Unlike these methods, Bee Go does not harm the bees or the environment, making it an ideal choice for beekeepers who want to protect both the bees and the ecosystem. Bee Go is typically used to move bees from one location to another, such as when relocating a hive or removing bees from a structure. The repellent is applied to a cotton pad or other absorbent material, which is then placed near the bees. The natural oils and scents in the repellent create an unpleasant odor that encourages the bees to move away from the treated area. Another benefit of using Bee Go is that it is easy to use and does not require any special equipment or training. Beekeepers and other professionals can apply the repellent themselves, without the need for expensive or complex equipment. Bee Go is a safe, natural, and effective bee repellent that is used by beekeepers and other professionals to move bees from one location to another. By using natural oils and scents to discourage bees from occupying a certain area, Bee Go provides a safe and environmentally friendly alternative to more traditional bee removal methods.

name: Bee Hive Net Cover
definition: A net covering that will support up to 4 story hives in both 8 frame and 10 frame sizes that is suitable for keeping bees in the hive during movement or for keeping them in the hive for safety reasons (e.g. pesticide spraying near the hive). Beehive net covers are made from a durable, breathable mesh material that allows air to circulate through the hive and is typically designed to fit over the top of the hive, providing a secure and snug fit that prevents predators from accessing the hive. The covers may also include additional features, such as zippers or drawstrings, that allow beekeepers to easily access the hive for inspection or maintenance.

name: Bee Hive Shim
definition: Bee hive shims are a necessity in the winter to allow extra space at the top of the hive for feeding dry sugar as an emergency winter food source for your bees, or for additional room for feeding winter patties. Most shims measure 3/4" tall and sit right on your top brood chamber. A hole can be drilled for top ventilation in the shim, if needed.

name: Bee Jacket
definition: A bee jacket is a specialized protective garment that is worn by beekeepers and other professionals when working with honey bees. The jacket is designed to protect the wearer from bee stings while also providing a comfortable and breathable environment for working with the bees. The bee jacket is typically made from a lightweight, breathable material that allows air to circulate through the garment. This helps to keep the wearer cool and comfortable, even in hot weather conditions. The jacket may also include additional features, such as mesh panels or ventilation flaps, to further improve air circulation and comfort. One of the key features of a bee jacket is its protective design. The jacket is typically made from a thick, durable material that protects from bee stings. The jacket may also include additional features, such as a high collar, elastic cuffs, and a zippered front, to further minimize the risk of bee stings. Another benefit of using a bee jacket is that it provides a clear visual signal to the bees. Bees are highly sensitive to movement and color and may become agitated or defensive if they perceive a threat. By wearing a bee jacket, beekeepers can help to reduce the risk of bee stings and other negative interactions with the bees.

name: Bee Lining
definition: Bee lining, also known as bee hunting, is a traditional method of locating wild honey bee colonies. The process involves using a combination of observation and tracking skills to locate the bees' flight path and follow it back to their hive. Bee lining typically involves setting up a bait station, such as a container filled with sugar water or honey, in an area where honey bees are known to be active. The beekeeper then observes the bees as they come and go from the bait station, looking for clues to their flight path and behavior. Once the beekeeper has identified the bees' flight path, they may use a variety of tracking methods to follow the bees back to their hive. This may include using a compass to track the direction of the flight path or marking the bees with colored powder to make them easier to follow. Bee lining requires a great deal of patience, skill, and observation, but can be a rewarding and sustainable way to locate wild honey bee colonies. By using natural methods to locate the bees, beekeepers can avoid the use of harmful pesticides or other chemicals and can help preserve the health and biodiversity of wild honey bee populations.

name: Bee mite
definition: a parasitic arthropod that infests honey bee colonies . See also “varroa mite” and “tracheal mite.”

name: Bee Quick
definition: Bee Quick is a specially designed chemical solution that is used by beekeepers to help remove honey from the hive without harming the bees or damaging the hive. The solution works by creating a scent that is unpleasant to the bees, encouraging them to move away from the honey supers (the boxes that hold the honeycombs), and allowing beekeepers to remove the honey without disrupting the hive. Bee Quick is typically applied to a special pad or cloth, usually in the form of a fume board, which is then placed on top of the honey supers. The scent of the solution encourages the bees to move away from the area, making it easier for beekeepers to remove the supers without harming the bees or causing too much disruption to the hive. One of the main benefits of using Bee Quick is that it provides a safe and non-toxic way to remove honey from the hive. Unlike more traditional methods of honey removal, such as using smoke or other disruptive methods, Bee Quick does not harm the bees or damage the hive, making it an ideal choice for beekeepers who want to protect both the bees and the environment. Another benefit of using Bee Quick is that it is easy to use and does not require any special equipment or training. Beekeepers can apply the solution themselves, without the need for expensive or complex equipment.

name: Bee Suit
definition: A bee suit is a specialized protective garment that is worn by beekeepers and other professionals when working with honey bees. The suit is designed to protect the wearer from bee stings while also providing a comfortable and breathable environment for working with the bees. The bee suit typically consists of several components, including a jacket or smock, pants, gloves, and a hat or veil. The jacket or smock is typically made from a thick, durable material that provides protection from bee stings, while the pants may include elastic cuffs or other features to prevent bees from getting inside. The gloves are typically made from a thick, heavy-duty material that provides protection for the hands and wrists, while the hat or veil is designed to protect the face and neck from bee stings. The hat or veil may be attached to the jacket or smock, or may be worn separately as a hood. One of the key features of a bee suit is its protective design. The suit is typically made from a thick, durable material that provides protection from bee stings. The suit may also include additional features, such as a high collar, elastic cuffs, and a zippered front, to further minimize the risk of bee stings. Another benefit of using a bee suit is that it provides a clear visual signal to the bees. Bees are highly sensitive to movement and color, and may become agitated or defensive if they perceive a threat. By wearing a bee suit, beekeepers can help to reduce the risk of bee stings and other negative interactions with the bees.

name: Bee Tree
definition: A bee tree is a term used to describe a tree or other natural structure that is home to a wild honey bee colony. Bee trees are typically located in forests, woodlands, or other natural environments, and may be difficult to find or access. Bee trees can be a valuable resource for beekeepers and other professionals who want to collect honey or preserve wild honey bee populations. However, locating and accessing a bee tree can be a challenging and time-consuming process, requiring specialized skills and equipment. One of the main challenges of working with bee trees is accessing the hive without harming the bees or damaging the tree. This may involve using specialized equipment, such as a tree-climbing harness or cherry picker, to reach the hive and collect the honey or relocate the bees. Bee trees can also be a valuable source of information for researchers and conservationists who want to study wild honey bee populations. By studying the behavior and biology of the bees within the tree, researchers can gain valuable insights into the health and sustainability of wild bee populations, and can develop strategies to protect and preserve these important pollinators.

name: Bee Veil
definition: A bee veil is a specialized piece of protective equipment that is worn by beekeepers and other professionals when working with honey bees. The veil is designed to protect the wearer's face and neck from bee stings while also providing visibility and breathability. The bee veil is typically made from a lightweight, breathable material that allows air to circulate through the garment. The veil may also include additional features, such as mesh panels or ventilation flaps, to further improve air circulation and comfort. One of the key features of a bee veil is its protective design. The veil typically includes a mesh panel that covers the face and neck, providing a barrier against bee stings while also allowing the wearer to see and breathe easily. The veil may also include additional features, such as a high collar and elastic cuffs, to further minimize the risk of bee stings. Another benefit of using a bee veil is that it provides a clear visual signal to the bees. Bees are highly sensitive to movement and color, and may become agitated or defensive if they perceive a threat. By wearing a bee veil, beekeepers can help to reduce the risk of bee stings and other negative interactions with the bees.

name: Bee Yard
definition: A bee yard, also known as an apiary, is a designated area where beekeepers keep their beehives. It is typically a fenced area that provides a safe and secure environment for bees to thrive and produce honey. A bee yard can consist of a few hives or several dozen, depending on the size of the operation. The hives are usually arranged in rows or clusters to make it easier for beekeepers to monitor and manage the colonies. Bee yards are often located in rural areas or near fields and orchards where bees can forage for nectar and pollen. They can be found all over the world, ranging from small backyard operations to large commercial apiaries. Bee yards are not only important for honey production, but also play a critical role in pollinating crops and maintaining the health of ecosystems.

name: Beehive
definition: a box or receptacle with movable frames, used for housing a colony of bees

name: Beekeeper
definition: A beekeeper is a person who manages bee colonies, typically in a bee yard or apiary, to produce honey, beeswax, and other products or services that bees provide. Beekeeping, also known as apiculture, is a centuries-old practice that requires knowledge, skill, and dedication. A beekeeper is responsible for ensuring the health and productivity of their bees. This includes maintaining the hives, monitoring the bees for signs of disease or pests, and providing them with adequate food and water. Beekeepers also need to manage the bees' reproductive cycles, ensuring that new queens are produced when necessary and preventing the bees from swarming. Beekeepers must be knowledgeable about bee behavior and biology, as well as the plants and flowers that bees rely on for nectar and pollen. They need to be able to identify different species of bees, as well as pests and diseases that can harm the colonies. Beekeepers must also be skilled in using various tools and equipment such as smokers, hive tools, and protective clothing. In addition to managing the bees themselves, beekeepers also need to be skilled in harvesting and processing honey, beeswax, and other products that the bees produce. This involves carefully removing the honeycomb from the hives, extracting the honey, and processing the wax. Beekeepers may also produce other products such as royal jelly, propolis, and pollen. Beekeeping is not just a profession, it is also a passion. Many beekeepers are dedicated to promoting the health and well-being of bees and are actively involved in bee conservation efforts. They may also provide education and outreach to the community, teaching others about the importance of bees and their role in our ecosystem. Overall, a beekeeper is a skilled and knowledgeable professional who is passionate about bees and their role in our world. They are responsible for managing bee colonies, producing honey and other products, and promoting the health and well-being of bees. Beekeeping is a challenging and rewarding profession that requires a deep understanding of bee behavior and biology, as well as a commitment to sustainability and conservation.

name: Beekeeping
definition: Beekeeping, also known as apiculture, is the process of managing bee colonies for the purpose of producing honey, beeswax, and other products that bees provide. The process of beekeeping typically begins with setting up a bee yard or apiary, which is a designated area where beekeepers keep their beehives. Beekeepers carefully select the location of their bee yard to ensure that the bees have access to a diverse range of nectar and pollen sources throughout the year. Once the bee yard is set up, beekeepers will introduce their bees to their new home. This typically involves purchasing a package of bees or a nucleus colony, which is a small starter colony consisting of a queen bee, worker bees, and some brood. Once the bees are settled into their new home, beekeepers will need to monitor their health and productivity on a regular basis. This includes maintaining the hives, checking for signs of disease or pests, and providing them with adequate food and water. Beekeepers also need to manage the bees' reproductive cycles, ensuring that new queens are produced when necessary and preventing the bees from swarming.

name: Benzaldehyde
definition: a volatile, almond-smelling chemical used to drive bees out of honey supers

name: Boot Bands
definition: Beekeepers use boot bands as a simple and effective way to protect their ankles and calves from bee stings while working with their colonies. Boot bands are typically made of heavy-duty elastic and are designed to fit snugly around the top of a beekeeper's boots or shoes, creating a barrier between the bees and the beekeeper's skin. To use boot bands, a beekeeper will typically slip them over the top of their boots or shoes before opening the hive. The tight fit of the bands prevents bees from crawling up the beekeeper's legs and prevents gaps between the beekeeper's clothing and their footwear, reducing the risk of bee stings. Boot bands are especially useful for beekeepers who are allergic to bee stings or who are working with particularly aggressive or defensive colonies. They are also useful for beginners who are still learning how to work with bees and may be more likely to make mistakes or disturb the bees. While boot bands are effective at preventing bee stings, beekeepers should still take other precautions to protect themselves when working with their colonies, such as wearing protective clothing, using a smoker to calm the bees, and approaching the hive slowly and calmly.

name: Bottling
definition: Filling glass or plastic bottles or jars with honey, using either gravity to fill the jars, or a honey bottling machine for more speed and consistency.

name: Bottling Tank
definition: The honey bottling tank is a cylindrical steel container that stands tall in the corner of the processing room. It has a capacity of 500 gallons and is designed to hold a large volume of honey before it is bottled and packaged for sale. The tank is equipped with a heating and cooling system that helps to maintain the ideal temperature for storing the honey. The tank is fitted with a stainless steel honey gate that allows for easy and precise control of the honey flow during the bottling process. The tank is an essential piece of equipment in any honey processing facility, ensuring that the honey is stored at the right temperature and quality is maintained throughout the bottling process.

name: Bottom Bar
definition: A bottom bar is an essential component of a bee hive frame that provides a sturdy foundation for the honeycomb. Made of high-quality wood, the bottom bar is designed to withstand the weight of the honey and the movement of the bees. It is typically rectangular in shape, with a smooth surface that allows for easy attachment of the wax foundation. The bottom bar is fitted with notches that help to keep the wax foundation in place, ensuring that the honeycomb is constructed properly. The bottom bar plays a crucial role in the health and productivity of the bee colony by providing a stable foundation for the bees to build their honeycomb. With its sturdy construction and precise design, the bottom bar is a key part of any well-functioning bee hive.

name: Bottom Supering
definition: Bottom supering is a technique used in beekeeping to provide additional space for honey production in a bee hive. It involves adding a new box or "super" to the bottom of the hive, rather than on top of the existing boxes. This method is particularly useful in areas where the honey flow is strong, as it allows the bees to quickly fill up the new super with honey. Bottom supering requires careful management and monitoring of the hive, as it can occasionally disrupt the natural brood cycle of the colony. However, when done correctly, it can greatly increase the overall honey yield of the hive. Beekeepers often use bottom supering in conjunction with other techniques, such as swarm prevention and hive splitting, to keep their bee colonies healthy and productive.

name: Brace/ burr comb
definition: bits of comb built between parallel combs, between comb and adjacent wood, or between two wooden parts such as top bars to fasten them together permitting workers to move easily within the nest

name: Breeder Hive
definition: A breeder hive is a special type of honey bee colony that is used for the selective breeding of bees. The hive is carefully managed to produce high-quality queen bees for use in other bee colonies. Breeder hives are typically established by beekeepers who are interested in improving the genetics of their bee colonies and producing bees that are better adapted to local conditions. The hive is populated with carefully selected bees that exhibit desirable traits, such as disease resistance and productivity. The queen bee is carefully monitored and evaluated for her egg-laying abilities and other characteristics that are important for the overall health and productivity of the hive. Breeder hives require careful management and monitoring, as they play a crucial role in the success of a beekeeping operation. By producing high-quality queen bees, breeder hives help to ensure the health and productivity of bee colonies and the overall success of the beekeeping industry.

name: Brood Comb
definition: Brood comb is a unique type of honeycomb in a bee hive designed to support the development of honey bee brood. It is composed of hexagonal cells that are slightly larger in size than those used for honey storage. These cells are specially crafted to accommodate the various developmental stages of the brood, from eggs to pupae. The queen bee plays a critical role in the development of the brood comb, laying her eggs in the cells of the brood comb. These eggs then receive the care and attention of the worker bees, who tend to them until they hatch into larvae. The larvae are then fed a nutritious blend of pollen and honey, providing them with the energy and nutrients required to grow and mature. The brood comb's hexagonal cells are shaped to perfection, ensuring maximum space utilization and efficient use of resources. Each cell is meticulously crafted with six sides, allowing for the perfect fit and the development of healthy brood. The cells' size is carefully calibrated to the developmental stage of the brood, with larger cells for the queen bee and smaller cells for worker bees and drones. Brood comb is a vital and indispensable component of a thriving bee colony. It is the foundation on which the future of the hive rests, representing the next generation of bees that will ensure the colony's survival and productivity. Beekeepers must carefully manage the brood comb to ensure that it is healthy and developing correctly, as any issues with the brood comb's development can lead to decreased productivity and even the collapse of the colony.

name: Brood Diseases
definition: Brood diseases are a group of infections that affect the developing stages of honey bees within a bee hive. These diseases can have a significant impact on the health and productivity of the colony, and can even lead to colony collapse if left untreated. The most common brood diseases include American Foulbrood, European Foulbrood, and Chalkbrood. American Foulbrood is caused by a bacterial infection and is characterized by a foul odor and a dark, sunken appearance of the brood comb. European Foulbrood is also caused by bacteria and is characterized by irregularly shaped cells and a yellowish-brown discoloration of the brood. Chalkbrood is caused by a fungus and is characterized by a white, chalky appearance of the brood. Beekeepers must carefully monitor their hives for signs of brood diseases and take measures to prevent their spread. This may include removing affected frames, sterilizing equipment, and treating the hive with antibiotics or other medications.

name: Brood Nest
definition: The honey bee brood nest refers to the area within a bee hive where the queen bee lays her eggs and the young brood develop. It is a critical component of a healthy and productive bee colony, representing the future generation of bees that will ensure the hive's survival and productivity. The brood nest is typically located in the center of the hive and is surrounded by storage cells for honey and pollen. It is carefully managed by worker bees, who maintain optimal temperature and humidity levels to ensure proper brood development. The nest is composed of frames that hold wax foundation, which the bees use to construct the honeycomb. The brood nest is comprised of three developmental stages of honey bees: eggs, larvae, and pupae. The queen bee lays the eggs, and the worker bees tend to them until they hatch into larvae. These larvae are then fed a nutritious blend of pollen and honey, promoting their growth and development. As the larvae mature, they spin cocoons around themselves, entering the pupal stage, during which they undergo metamorphosis and transform into adult bees. Effective management and monitoring of the brood nest are essential to ensure the health and productivity of the hive. Beekeepers must regularly inspect the brood nest to ensure that the brood is healthy and developing correctly. Issues with brood development can indicate underlying problems within the hive, such as disease, malnutrition, or overcrowding. If left unaddressed, these issues can lead to decreased productivity and even the collapse of the colony.

name: Bucket Bench
definition: A bucket bench for honey extraction is a highly practical and efficient tool that is used to transfer honey from one bucket to another. Typically made out of heavy-duty plastic or solid stainless steel, this tool is designed to hold one bucket at a slight angle above another, so that the honey can be easily drained into the second bucket. The plastic bucket benches are designed to be taken apart into three pieces for easier clean up and storage. The bucket bench is equipped with a series of notches and grooves that are specifically designed to securely hold the honey bucket in place while it is being tilted. This ensures that the honey flows smoothly and evenly from the top bucket to the bottom one. The slanted angle of the bench allows the honey to flow quickly and efficiently, while the sturdy construction ensures that the bench remains stable and secure throughout the extraction process. One of the major advantages of using a bucket bench is that it minimizes the risk of honey spillage or waste. The tool is designed to be easy to use and to facilitate the honey extraction process in a clean, efficient, and hassle-free manner. The plastic bucket benches can be disassembled into three pieces, making it easy to clean and store when not in use. Overall, a bucket bench is an essential tool for anyone involved in honey extraction. It is highly practical, easy to use, and designed to make the honey extraction process as efficient and stress-free as possible.

name: Buckfast Bees
definition: Buckfast bees are a hybrid of honeybees that were first created by Brother Adam, a monk at Buckfast Abbey in Devon, England, in the early 20th century. These bees were created by crossbreeding various strains of honeybees from around the world, including Italian, Caucasian, and Carniolan bees. The resulting hybrid bees are known for their gentle disposition, high productivity, and resistance to disease, making them a popular choice among beekeepers worldwide. Buckfast bees are known for their exceptional honey production, as well as their ability to pollinate crops and plants. They are highly adaptable to a wide range of climates and can thrive in both warm and cold environments. The Buckfast bee is also highly resistant to Varroa mites, which are a major pest for many other breeds of honeybees. This makes them a popular choice among beekeepers who are looking for a low-maintenance, high-yield bee. One of the unique features of Buckfast bees is their distinctive coloring. They are typically a light golden brown color, with yellow and brown stripes on their bodies. They are also known for their long tongues, which allow them to collect nectar and pollen from a wide range of flowers. Buckfast bees are highly prized by beekeepers for their docile and friendly temperament, which makes them easy to handle and work with. Overall, Buckfast bees are a popular choice among beekeepers worldwide due to their high productivity, gentle temperament, and resistance to disease. They are a testament to the ingenuity and skill of Brother Adam, who spent decades perfecting the breeding of these remarkable hybrid bees.

name: Build Up
definition: Build up is a term used to describe the process by which a honey bee colony increases in size and population in preparation for the upcoming season. This process typically occurs in the spring, when the weather begins to warm up and the bees become more active. During this time, the queen bee will begin to lay eggs at a faster rate, and the worker bees will start to collect more nectar and pollen to feed the growing larvae. As the colony begins to build up, the bees will also start to produce more honey. This honey will be stored in the comb and used to feed the bees during times when nectar and pollen are scarce, such as during the winter months. The build up process is crucial for the survival of the colony, as it ensures that there are enough bees and resources to sustain the hive throughout the year. However, build up can also have its downsides. If the colony becomes too large too quickly, it can lead to overcrowding and swarming. Swarming occurs when the queen and a portion of the worker bees leave the hive to form a new colony elsewhere, leaving the original colony weakened and vulnerable. To prevent swarming, beekeepers will often split the colony or add additional supers to the hive, allowing the bees more space to build up without becoming overcrowded. Overall, build up is an important process for the health and survival of a honey bee colony, and it requires careful monitoring and management to ensure that the bees thrive.

name: Burlap Smoker Fuel
definition: Burlap bee smoker fuel is a common type of smoker fuel used by beekeepers to calm honey bees during hive inspections and honey harvesting. The fuel is made from strips or squares of burlap cloth that are rolled or twisted into tight bundles, which are then ignited and placed in the smoker. The smoke produced by burning burlap is cool and thick, which helps to calm the bees and make them less defensive. One of the advantages of using burlap bee smoker fuel is that it is readily available and inexpensive. Beekeepers can easily obtain burlap cloth from hardware stores or online retailers, and it is easy to prepare for use in a smoker. The thick, cool smoke produced by burning burlap is also less likely to harm the bees or damage the honeycomb than hot, acrid smoke produced by other types of fuel.

name: Candle Molds
definition: Candle molds are containers designed to shape and hold wax as it cools and solidifies into a candle shape. They come in various shapes and sizes, allowing for a wide range of candle designs. Some candle molds are designed for a specific type of candle, such as votive candles or pillar candles, while others can be used to create a variety of candle shapes. Some molds are one-piece, while others have multiple pieces that need to be assembled before pouring in the wax.

name: Candle Wick Tabs
definition: Candle wick tabs are small metal pieces that are used to secure the wick to the bottom of a candle container. They are typically made from either zinc or stainless steel, which are both durable and resistant to corrosion. The tabs are designed to hold the wick in place and prevent it from moving around during the candle-making process. They are also important for ensuring that the wick stays centered in the candle, which is essential for even burning and maximum fragrance throw.

name: Candy Plug
definition: Queen cage candy plugs are small pieces of candy that are used to release a queen bee from her cage. When a beekeeper introduces a new queen bee to a colony, they will often place her in a small cage to prevent her from immediately being attacked by the other bees. The cage usually has a candy plug on one end, which the bees will eat through over time to release the queen. The candy plug serves as a slow-release mechanism for the queen bee. As the bees eat through the candy, they become accustomed to the queen's pheromones and are more likely to accept her as their new queen. This process can take several days, during which time the queen is protected from the other bees in the colony. Queen cage candy plugs are typically made from a mixture of sugar, honey, and water. The candy is formed into a small plug that fits into the end of the queen cage. The size of the plug is important, as it must be large enough to prevent the queen from escaping, but soft enough for the bees to eat through. Beekeepers must be careful when using queen cage candy plugs, as they can attract other insects or animals to the hive. Ants, in particular, are known to be attracted to the sweet smell of the candy and may attempt to invade the hive. Beekeepers may need to use ant traps or other methods to keep the ants away. In summary, queen cage candy plugs are an important tool for beekeepers when introducing a new queen bee to a colony. They provide a slow-release mechanism that helps the bees become accustomed to the queen's pheromones, and they also protect the queen from the other bees in the colony. However, beekeepers must be careful to use them properly to avoid attracting unwanted pests to the hive

name: Capped Honey
definition: Capped honey is a product of honeybees that has undergone a process called capping. Honeybees collect nectar from flowers and convert it into honey through a process called regurgitation and evaporation. Once the honey is ready, the bees store it in wax cells called honeycomb. To preserve the honey, the bees cover the cells with a thin layer of wax called capping. This capping seals the honey inside the honeycomb and protects it from moisture and air. Capped honey is distinguished by its opaque appearance, which is due to the wax capping that covers the honey. The wax capping can be removed to access the honey inside, which can be used for a variety of purposes, such as sweetening food and drinks, making desserts, or as a natural remedy for coughs and sore throats. Capped honey is considered to be of high quality, as it is a sign that the honey has matured and is ready for consumption. Honeybees cap the honey only when they determine that the water content of the honey is low enough to prevent fermentation. As a result, capped honey has a longer shelf life than uncapped honey. Overall, capped honey is a delicious and nutritious natural product that is enjoyed by people worldwide. Its unique appearance and distinctive flavor make it a popular ingredient in many recipes, and its health benefits make it a valuable addition to any diet.

name: Capping Melter
definition: A beekeeping capping melter is a specialized piece of equipment used in the process of harvesting honey from beehives. When beekeepers extract honey from the comb, they first need to remove a thin layer of wax that covers the honey cells. This layer is called the capping, and it needs to be melted off before the honey can be extracted. A capping melter is specifically designed to melt the wax cappings so that the honey can be released. It consists of a large metal drum or container that is heated by electricity or gas. The cappings are placed inside the drum, and the heat causes the wax to melt and drip down into a separate container below. Some capping melters have a built-in filtration system that separates the melted wax from any residual honey, making it easy to collect and reuse the wax for other purposes. The melted wax can be used to make candles or other beeswax products, while the honey can be processed and jarred for sale or personal use. Using a capping melter is an efficient and effective way to harvest honey without damaging the comb. It also ensures that the wax can be reused, reducing waste and making beekeeping a more sustainable practice.

name: Capping Scratcher
definition: A capping scratcher is specifically designed to remove the wax cappings without damaging the comb. It consists of a small metal tool with sharp teeth on one end. The beekeeper uses the teeth to gently scratch the wax cappings off the honey cells, exposing the honey underneath. The capping scratcher is a simple but effective tool that is widely used in beekeeping. It is easy to use and does not require any special skills or training. It is also relatively inexpensive, making it accessible to beekeepers of all levels. Using a capping scratcher is an important step in the process of harvesting honey. It ensures that the comb remains intact, which is important for the health and wellbeing of the bees. It also allows beekeepers to harvest honey without damaging the comb, making it easier to reuse the comb in future honey harvests.

name: Cappings Spinner
definition: beekeeping capping spinner is a specialized piece of equipment used for harvesting honey from beehives. When beekeepers extract honey from the comb, they first need to remove the wax cappings that cover the honey cells. This is typically done using a heated knife or capping melter. Once the cappings are removed, the honey can be extracted using a capping spinner. A capping spinner is a machine that uses centrifugal force to extract honey from the comb. It consists of a drum or basket that holds the frames of honeycomb horizontally. The frames are spun rapidly, which causes the honey to fly out of the comb and collect on the inner walls of the drum or basket. The honey then flows down to the bottom of the machine, where it can be collected in a separate container. The capping spinner is designed to be gentle on the honeycomb, so the bees can reuse it for future honey production. After the honey is extracted, the beekeeper can return the frames to the hive, and the bees will clean them and fill them with honey again. Using a capping spinner is a fast and efficient way to extract honey from the comb. It reduces the amount of time and labor required to harvest honey and minimizes the damage to the honeycomb. It also ensures that the honey is collected in a clean and sanitary manner, ready for processing and jarring for sale or personal use.

name: Carniolan Bees
definition: Carniolan bees, also known as Carniolans, are a subspecies of the Western honey bee (Apis mellifera). They are named after their region of origin, Carniola, which is now part of Slovenia. Carniolan bees are highly valued by beekeepers for their gentle temperament, high productivity, and ability to adapt to different environmental conditions. One of the most distinctive features of Carniolan bees is their greyish-black coloring, which sets them apart from other subspecies of honey bees. They are slightly smaller than other honey bees, but are known for their rapid spring build-up and early honey production. They are excellent foragers and can collect nectar and pollen from a wide range of plants, making them well-suited for different types of environments. Carniolan bees are also highly adaptable to different climatic conditions. They are known for their ability to withstand colder temperatures and longer winters compared to other honey bee subspecies. They are also resistant to diseases and pests, making them an attractive option for beekeepers who want to avoid using chemicals or pesticides. Another characteristic of Carniolan bees is their gentle temperament. They are known for being less aggressive than other honey bee subspecies, which makes them easier to work with for beekeepers and less threatening to the general public. However, it is important to note that all bees can become agitated if they feel threatened or if their hive is disturbed. Overall, Carniolan bees are a popular choice for beekeepers due to their productivity, adaptability, and gentle nature. They are widely distributed throughout Europe and North America, and many beekeepers consider them to be one of the best subspecies of honey bees.

name: Castes
definition: Honey bees have three castes: workers, drones, and the queen. Workers perform various tasks, drones mate with a queen, and the queen lays eggs and regulates the colony. Each caste plays a crucial role in the survival and productivity of the colony.

name: Caucasian Bees
definition: Caucasian honey bees, also known as Caucasian Mountain bees, are a subspecies of honey bees that are native to the Caucasus region, which includes parts of Russia, Georgia, Azerbaijan, and Armenia. They are known for their gentle behavior, high honey production, and disease resistance, which makes them popular among beekeepers. Caucasian honey bees have a unique appearance, with yellow-brown stripes on their abdomen and a darker thorax. They are slightly larger than other common honey bee subspecies, with a body length of around 15-17mm. They are also known for their long tongues, which allow them to collect nectar from deep flowers. These bees are highly adaptable to different environments and can thrive in a variety of climates. They are excellent foragers and can travel long distances in search of nectar and pollen. They are also known for their ability to produce honey with a low water content, which makes it less likely to ferment and spoil. One of the most notable characteristics of Caucasian honey bees is their gentle temperament. They are less likely to sting than other subspecies, which makes them easier to handle and manage. They are also less likely to swarm, which is a common behavior among other honey bee subspecies. In terms of honey production, Caucasian honey bees are highly productive. They can produce up to 200 pounds of honey per hive per year, which is significantly higher than other subspecies. The honey they produce is light in color and has a mild, pleasant flavor. Overall, Caucasian honey bees are a popular choice among beekeepers due to their adaptability, disease resistance, and gentle behavior. They are an excellent choice for both commercial and hobby beekeeping operations, and their high honey production makes them a valuable asset for honey producers.

name: Cell Bar
definition: A cell bar is a small wooden frame that is used in the process of queen rearing. It usually measures about 6 inches long and 1 inch wide, and it has a series of holes drilled into it. These holes are designed to hold queen rearing cups, which are small plastic or wax cups that are used to rear queen bees. The cell bar is usually placed in a queenless hive, where the worker bees will start to build queen cells in the cups. Once these cells are capped, they can be removed from the cell bar and transferred to a mating nuc or queenless colony, where they will hatch into new queen bees. Cell bars are a popular tool for beekeepers who want to produce their own queens, as they allow for a high degree of control over the queen rearing process.

name: Cell Cup
definition: A cell cup for queen rearing is a small plastic or wax cup used by beekeepers to raise queen bees. These cups are typically around 10mm in height and have a narrow opening at the top, which allows the bees to deposit royal jelly and eggs inside. The bottom of the cup is usually tapered to fit into a specially designed frame, which holds multiple cups in place. Once the eggs and royal jelly are deposited, the beekeeper will move the cups to a queenless colony, where the workers will raise the queen larvae. After several days, the beekeeper can remove the cups and transfer the queen cells to a new colony. Overall, cell cups are an essential tool for beekeepers looking to raise new queen bees and maintain healthy hives.

name: Cell Starter
definition: A cell starter hive for queen rearing is a specialized beehive used by beekeepers to encourage bees to create queen cells. This hive typically consists of a standard brood box and one or more honey supers, with a specialized cell starter frame positioned in the brood box. The cell starter frame is designed to hold a series of queen cups or cell cups, and is usually equipped with a plastic or metal queen excluder to prevent the queen from laying eggs in the cups. The cell starter hive is typically populated with a strong, healthy colony of bees, which is encouraged to create queen cells through a variety of management techniques, such as removing the queen or providing a source of artificial swarming. The hive is also designed to provide the bees with optimal conditions for queen cell production, such as warmth, humidity, and an ample supply of food. Once the queen cells are ready to be moved, the beekeeper will remove the cell starter hive and transfer the queen cells to a queenless colony for further development. Overall, a cell starter hive is an essential tool for beekeepers looking to raise new queen bees and maintain healthy hives.

name: Chain Uncapper
definition: A chain uncapper is a piece of equipment used in beekeeping to remove the wax caps from honeycomb frames before extracting honey. It features a rotating chain made up of small metal teeth that gently scrape away the wax caps from both sides of the frame. The chain is powered by an electric motor, which rotates the chain around a drum. The chain of the uncapper is typically made of high-quality stainless steel, which is durable and long-lasting. The teeth of the chain are carefully designed to be sharp enough to remove the wax caps, but not so sharp as to damage the honeycomb. The chain is usually around 2 inches wide and is long enough to accommodate multiple frames at once. The beekeeper places the honeycomb frames on a support structure, which is usually a metal bar that holds the frames in place. The frames are then passed through the rotating chain, which gently removes the wax caps from both sides of the frame. The removed wax falls through the uncapper and into a bin or tray below. Chain uncappers are commonly used in commercial beekeeping operations to process large quantities of honey quickly and efficiently. They are also used by hobbyist beekeepers who want to extract honey without damaging the honeycomb. They are relatively easy to use, and the uncapping process can be completed quickly with minimal effort. Overall, a chain uncapper is an essential tool for beekeepers who want to extract honey without damaging the delicate wax comb. They are durable, efficient, and easy to use, making them a popular choice for beekeepers of all levels of experience.

name: Chalkbrood Disease
definition: A disease caused by the fungus Ascosphaera apis . This fungus produces spores which are swallowed by honey bee larvae when they are fed by nurse bees. The spores germinate in the gut of the larvae as it is developing, and ultimately cause the larvae to die of starvation. It is more common in the spring during the cool wet weather, when the brood nest is quickly expanding and a smaller adult workforce cannot maintain brood nest temperature. Infected hives have a scattered brood pattern with perforated cappings. Larvae infected with chalkbrood disease will usually die after their cell has been capped, so the fungus will grow to fill the cell. The cappings of cells containing dead larvae most often will be chewed away by the worker bees of the hive and the mummified bodies of the larvae will be removed to the hive entrance or dropped on the ground outside of the hive. Chalkbrood disease can easily be spread between hives through the drifting behavior of the drones, as well as the robbing tendencies of the worker bees. Once inside a hive, fungal spores are quickly spread throughout the hive from the mummified bodies. It can also be transferred between apiaries by contaminated equipment.

name: Checkerboarding
definition: Checkerboarding is an excellent, non-invasive swarm management technique. If the hive becomes strong enough, with enough honey stores, then it will start swarm preparations. There are several conditions that will make a hive swarm, and one of these conditions is the amount of food storage that is above the brood nest. If there is no more room for food storage, the bees will decide to swarm. By going in and alternating the filled honey frames with empty frames in the boxes above the brood nest, you will give the bees a reason to decide to stay and finish filling up the empty frames, instead of swarming. The end result will be that you have a bigger, stronger colony of bees that have produced a lot of honey.

name: Chimney
definition: The process of the bees filling only the center frames of the honey supers. This can be fixed manually by the beekeeper, by adding empty frames in between the filled frames of the honey supers, also called "checkerboarding".

name: Chinese Grafting Tool
definition: A tool made of bamboo or plastic that has a retractable tongue to slide under the bee larvae to pick it up. When released, the larvae gets pushed off the tongue, into a cell cup to be used for queen rearing. This type of grafting tool is easier to operate than most other types, plus it keeps a lot of royal jelly with the larvae during the process.

name: Clarifying
definition: The process of clarifying honey involves removing any impurities and debris from the honey, resulting in a clear and smooth consistency. This is typically done by heating the honey to a specific temperature and then filtering it through a fine mesh or cloth to remove any remaining particles. The heat helps to break down any crystals or wax that may be present, making it easier to strain out the impurities. Once the honey has been clarified, it can be stored in a clean, airtight container at room temperature for several months. It's worth noting that while clarifying honey can improve its appearance and texture, it may also remove some of the beneficial enzymes and nutrients that make raw honey a popular health food.

name: Cleansing Flights
definition: Cleansing flights are an important behavior of honey bees, particularly in the winter months when they are confined to their hives. Bees use these flights to eliminate waste and debris from their bodies, which they are unable to do inside the hive. During a cleansing flight, bees will fly out of the hive and defecate, as well as remove any dead bees or other debris that has accumulated within the hive. These flights are crucial for maintaining the cleanliness and health of the hive, and can occur on warmer days throughout the winter months.

name: Clipped Wings
definition: Something some beekeepers do to the queen to prevent her from swarming and flying off. Bees use queens wings as a determinate of how old the queen is, and can kill and replace a queen with clipped wings, even if she is brand new.

name: Cloak Board, Cloaking Board
definition: A cloak board is a useful tool for beekeepers that is placed over the top of a honey bee colony during the harvest season. It is used to allow the beekeeper to remove honey from the hive without disrupting the bees or causing harm to the colony. The cloak board effectively creates a separate space between the honey supers and the rest of the hive, allowing the beekeeper to remove the supers and extract honey without disturbing the bees or exposing them to light. The board is typically made of wood or plastic, and is placed on top of the colony after the honey supers are removed. It helps to create a darker and more secure environment for the bees, which reduces their stress levels and minimizes the risk of colony collapse. A cloak board can also be used for queen rearing in beekeeping. It is used to temporarily separate the queen from the rest of the hive, providing an environment where new queen cells can be introduced and developed without the risk of the existing queen destroying them. To use a cloak board for queen rearing, the beekeeper will remove the queen from the hive and place her under the cloak board, which is then placed over the top of the hive. This separates the queen from the rest of the colony, allowing the beekeeper to introduce queen cells into the hive. The bees will then start to care for the queen cells and raise new queens, which can be used to replace an old or failing queen or to create a new colony. The cloak board helps to keep the queen in a safe and secure environment, while also allowing the beekeeper to monitor the progress of the queen rearing process.

name: Cocoon
definition: A bee cocoon, also known as a pupa, is the stage in a bee's development when it transforms from a larva into an adult bee. During this stage, the bee is encased in a protective cocoon made from silk that it produces itself. The cocoon is typically a light brown color and shaped like a small, oblong capsule. Inside the cocoon, the bee undergoes metamorphosis, developing wings, legs, and other body parts that will allow it to fly and collect nectar and pollen. After about two weeks, the adult bee emerges from the cocoon and begins its life as a worker bee, queen bee, or drone, depending on its sex and role in the hive. .

name: Cold Uncapping Knife
definition: A cold uncapping knife is a tool used in beekeeping to remove the wax caps that bees create to seal honeycomb cells. Unlike a hot knife, which uses heat to melt the wax, a cold uncapping knife has a serrated blade that allows beekeepers to scrape away the wax without damaging the honeycomb or exposing the honey to excess heat. The blade of a cold uncapping knife is typically made of stainless steel, and the handle may be made of wood or a heat-resistant plastic material. Cold uncapping knives come in different sizes and shapes, and some models have a comb-like edge that helps beekeepers to remove the wax caps more efficiently. Overall, a cold uncapping knife is an essential tool for beekeepers who want to harvest honey from their hives while minimizing damage to their bees and honeycomb.

name: Colony Collapse Disorder
definition: Colony collapse disorder (CCD) is a phenomenon that occurs when the majority of worker bees in a honeybee colony disappear and leave behind a queen, plenty of food, and a few nurse bees to care for the remaining immature bees and the queen. The cause of colony collapse disorder is not fully understood, but it is believed to be a combination of factors, including pesticides, parasites, disease, and stress on the bees' immune system. The loss of honeybees due to colony collapse disorder is a major concern for agriculture because honeybees are crucial pollinators for many crops

name: Comb Capper
definition: A Comb Capper is a very important tool to use when harvesting honey in beekeeping. The Comb Capper features a built in groove in the bottom to hold the unit in place on the bucket, as well as a place in the top to hold a 6-5/8" honey super frame, or a 9-5/8" frame. The Comb Capper holds the frame steady to make an easier job of uncapping for the beekeeper!

name: Comb Foundation
definition: Comb foundation is a thin sheet of beeswax that is used in beekeeping to provide a foundation for bees to build their honeycomb. It is created by pressing a pattern of hexagonal cells into sheets of beeswax. The hexagonal pattern mimics the natural structure of honeycomb, which allows bees to build their comb more easily and quickly. The sheets of comb foundation come in different sizes and thicknesses, depending on the needs of the beekeeper and the type of hive they are using. They can be purchased pre-formed, or beekeepers can create their own by using a comb foundation mill. When beekeepers insert comb foundation into a hive, they attach it to frames and provide bees with a starting point for building their comb. Bees will then use the comb foundation as a guide and continue building their honeycomb around it. This makes it easier for beekeepers to manage their hives, as they can easily inspect the comb and harvest honey without damaging the structure. Comb foundation is an important component in beekeeping, as it helps ensure the healthy growth and productivity of bee colonies. It also serves as the foundation for the production of honey and other bee products.

name: Comb Honey Cutter
definition: A comb honey cutter is a square piece of food grade stainless steel, used to cut a frame of comb honey down into smaller sections, to make it easier to package the comb honey for resale.

name: Complete Beehive Kit
definition: A complete hive kit typically includes a bottom board, one or two deep hive bodies, frames, foundation, inner cover, outer cover, and a queen excluder. The bottom board serves as the base of the hive and provides ventilation. The deep hive bodies are stacked on the bottom board and are where the bees build their comb and store honey and pollen. Frames and foundation are inserted into the hive bodies and provide a structure for the bees to build their comb. The inner cover sits on top of the hive bodies and provides insulation and ventilation. The outer cover protects the hive from the elements. The queen excluder is a mesh screen that keeps the queen bee from laying eggs in certain parts of the hive. All of these components work together to create a safe and functional home for a colony of bees.

name: Conical Escape
definition: A cone shaped bee escape, which permits bees a one way exit out of the honey supers when used on a special escape board.

name: Cordovan Bees
definition: Cordovan Italian bees are a subspecies of the Italian honey bee. They are known for their distinct coloration, which is a light golden or yellowish-brown hue. This coloration is a result of their genetics, which differ from those of the standard Italian honey bee. Cordovan Italian bees are known to be gentle and easy to work with, which makes them a popular choice for beekeepers. They are also known to be highly productive, producing large amounts of honey and beeswax. Additionally, they are resistant to many common honey bee pests and diseases. One of the unique features of Cordovan Italian bees is their ability to perform a behavior known as "swarming." Swarming occurs when a large group of bees, including the queen, leave the hive in search of a new location to build a nest. While this behavior can be concerning for beekeepers, it is a natural part of the honey bee life cycle and can be managed with proper care and attention. Overall, Cordovan Italian bees are a great choice for beekeepers who want a productive, gentle, and disease-resistant honey bee subspecies.

name: Creamed (Crystallized) honey
definition: honey that has been allowed to crystallize, usually under con- trolled conditions, to produce a tiny crystal that gives the honey a creamy texture

name: Crimp Wired Foundation
definition: Crimped wire foundation is a type of foundation used in beekeeping to support honeycomb. It is made by crimping thin wires in a specific pattern and embedding them in beeswax. The crimped wires provide strength and support to the honeycomb, preventing it from collapsing and allowing bees to build straight and uniform comb. Crimped wire foundation is commonly used in modern beekeeping and is considered an essential component of a beehive.

name: Crimp-wired foundation
definition: comb foundation into which thin crimped wire is embedded vertically during foundation manufacture

name: Cross Pollination
definition: Cross-pollination is a process in which pollen from one plant is transferred to the stigma (female reproductive part) of another plant, leading to fertilization and the production of new seeds. This can happen through various means, such as wind, insects, birds, or other animals that carry pollen from one flower or plant to another. Cross-pollination is important in many plant species as it leads to genetic diversity, which can result in healthier and more robust plants. It can also lead to the creation of new plant varieties that exhibit desirable characteristics, such as resistance to disease or pests

name: Cut Comb Honey
definition: Comb honey cut into various sizes, the edges drained, and the pieces wrapped individually.

name: Cut Out
definition: A cut out is a process of removing an established beehive from a structure, such as a wall, floor, or ceiling, and relocating it to a new location. This process is typically performed by professional beekeepers or pest control specialists, as it requires specialized equipment and expertise. Performing a cut out involves carefully removing the honeycomb frames from the structure, and then relocating the bees and their queen to a new hive box. Once the bees and their comb have been moved, the area where the hive was located may need to be repaired or sealed to prevent future bee infestations. Cut outs are a humane and eco-friendly way to deal with a bee infestation, as it preserves the bees and allows them to continue their important role in pollinating plants and producing honey.

name: Dancing
definition: a series of repeated movements of bees on comb; round and wag-tail (or waggling) dance are used to communicate the location of food sources and potential home sites

name: Decoy hive
definition: see “bait hive.”

name: Deep Brood Box
definition: A deep brood box is a type of beehive box that is used to house the colony's brood (eggs, larvae, and pupae) and the queen. It is typically the largest box in a beehive and is usually placed at the bottom of the hive. A deep brood box can hold up to 10 frames, and the frames are filled with comb that the bees use to store their brood and honey. The deep brood box is an essential component of a beehive and is necessary for the survival and health of the bee colony.

name: Deformed Wing Virus
definition: Deformed Wing Virus (DWV) is a highly contagious and widespread virus that affects honeybees. It is transmitted by Varroa mites, which feed on the bees' hemolymph (blood). DWV can cause deformed wings and other physical deformities in infected bees, making it difficult for them to fly and forage for food. It can also weaken the immune system of bees, making them more susceptible to other diseases and infections. DWV is a major concern for beekeepers, as it can lead to significant losses in bee populations and ultimately impact the pollination of crops.

name: Demaree
definition: the method of swarm control that separates the queen from most of the brood within the same hive

name: Dequeen
definition: "Dequeening" a beehive refers to the process of removing the queen bee from the hive. This is usually done when the beekeeper wants to replace the existing queen with a new queen or to prevent the hive from swarming. The process involves locating the queen and either removing her manually or using a specialized tool to trap and remove her. Once the queen is dequeened, the bees in the hive will start the process of creating a new queen by selecting a new larvae and feeding it a special diet of royal jelly. Dequeening can be a delicate process and should only be attempted by experienced beekeepers.

name: Detritus
definition: Detritus on bees refers to the accumulation of debris, dust, and other particles on the body of bees. This can include pollen, wax, propolis, and other materials that the bees come into contact with during their foraging activities. Detritus can be harmful to bees as it can interfere with their ability to fly, groom themselves, and regulate their body temperature. It can also make them more susceptible to diseases and parasites. Beekeepers often monitor the detritus levels on their bees as an indicator of their overall health and well-being.

name: Dextrose Sugar
definition: Also known as glucose; one of the two principal sugars found in honey, it is the sugar that forms crystals during granulation. It is one of the most common types of sugars used in food and beverage production due to its sweetness and ability to dissolve easily in water. Dextrose sugar is considered a natural sweetener and is often used as a substitute for refined sugars in health-conscious diets.

name: Diatomaceous Earth
definition: Diatomaceous Earth is a naturally occurring sedimentary rock that is made up of the fossilized remains of ancient aquatic organisms called diatoms. It is a fine, powdery substance that is rich in silica and has a number of uses in various industries. Diatomaceous Earth is commonly used in agriculture as a natural pesticide and soil conditioner. It is also used in the food and beverage industry as a filtration aid, and in the cosmetics industry as an exfoliant and absorbent. Diatomaceous Earth is used in beekeeping by sprinkling some outside the hive on the ground to help control small hive beetles. Diatomaceous Earth can also be used as a filtration material for cleaning beeswax.

name: Disease Resistance
definition: Disease resistance in honey bees is an important topic in beekeeping. Honey bees are susceptible to various diseases, including American foulbrood, European foulbrood, chalkbrood, and nosema. These diseases can weaken the bees, affect their lifespan, and even cause colony collapse disorder. Beekeepers can improve disease resistance in their hives by maintaining good hygiene practices, monitoring the health of their bees, and treating any diseases promptly with appropriate medications. Additionally, breeding programs that focus on selecting for disease-resistant traits can help to improve the overall health and resilience of honey bee populations.

name: Disposable Beetle Traps
definition: The disposable beetle traps work great at preventing hive beetle infestation. Simply fill the traps half way full with food grade oil or apple cider vinegar, place between the outer frames of your brood boxes. The beetles will either be lured to the trap by the smell of the vinegar, or they will hide from the bees in the traps and not be able to get back out. There is no messy clean up with the disposable beetle traps; simply throw away and use a new one!

name: Double Drain Tank
definition: This tank helps you harvest more honey from your wax. The tank holds the cappings above a large grate, allowing them to drain down into the bottom tank, where it can easily be drained into a filter from the honey gate located in the side of the tank.

name: Double Story
definition: A double-story beehive is a type of beehive that consists of two boxes stacked on top of each other. Each box contains a set of frames where the bees can build their honeycomb and store their honey and brood. The bottom box is usually used for brood rearing, while the top box is used for honey storage. This type of beehive allows beekeepers to harvest honey without disturbing the brood chamber, and it also provides more space for the bees to expand their colony. However, it's important to note that a double-story hive can be heavier and more difficult to manage than a single-story hive, so it's best to ensure that you have the necessary equipment and knowledge before using this type of beehive.

name: Drawn Comb
definition: Drawn comb refers to the beeswax comb that has been created by the bees. It is a natural comb that is used by bees to store honey, pollen, and brood. Drawn comb is important for beekeepers as it provides a foundation for the bees to build upon and helps to maintain the structure of the hive. It also allows for easier inspection and management of the hive. Drawn comb can be reused by the bees for several years, but it should be cleaned and sterilized between uses to prevent the spread of disease.

name: Drifting of bees
definition: the failure of bees to return to their own hive in an apiary containing many colonies. Young bees tend to drift more than older bees, and bees from small colonies tend to drift into larger colonies

name: Drifting of Honey Bees
definition: When bees are first installed in hives from packages (and you have more than 1 package that you are installing), or when multiple bee hives are moved from place to place, bees can become confused and will not know which hive they have come out of, and will return to the wrong hive. Most often times the hives they return to are on the ends. This will add more population to the other hives, but can drastically impair the hives in the middle of the row.

name: Drone Bees
definition: The male honey bees that come from an unfertilized egg laid by a queen. During the fall, the worker bees will kick the drones out of the hive because they are not needed anymore, and will just eat up extra stores of honey if left in the hive over the winter.

name: Drone Brood
definition: Drone brood is a term used to describe the cells of the honeycomb where male bees, or drones, are raised. These cells are larger than the cells used for female bees, or worker bees, and are capped for a longer period of time. Drones are important for the reproduction of the hive, as they mate with the queen to produce new colonies. However, too many drones can be detrimental to the hive, as they consume resources without contributing to the work of the colony. If you see an overabundance of these cells in your hive, it could be due to having an injured or unfertilized queen who is unable to lay fertilized eggs. Beekeepers often monitor the amount of drone brood in their hives to ensure the health and productivity of the colony.

name: Drone Congregation Area
definition: Drones from hives in many surrounding areas meet here and wait for a virgin queen to arrive.

name: Drone Layer; Drone Laying Queen
definition: The drone laying queen is a queen who is not mated or who has been injured and is incapable of laying fertilized eggs. As a result, all the brood produced by her will be drones.

name: Drumming
definition: rhythmic pounding on the sides of a hive to make the bees ascend into another box/hive body placed over it

name: Drums of Honey
definition: Bulk honey produced by large scale beekeepers can be sold in 55 gallon steel barrels. One drum of honey can hold 650 - 700 pounds of pure honey.

name: Duragilt Foundation
definition: Duragilt foundation is an older style of foundation not easily found anymore. It is a sheet of clear, thin plastic that has a light coating of beeswax on each side.

name: Dwindling
definition: the rapid dying off of old bees in the spring; sometimes called spring dwindling or disappearing disease (because a pathogen may be involved)

name: Electric embedder
definition: a device allowing rapid embedding of wires in foundation with electrically produced heat

name: European foulbrood (EFB)
definition: an infectious brood disease of honey bees caused by the bacterium Melissococcus (formally Streptococcus) pluton

name: Extender (grease) patty
definition: a mixture of vegetable shortening and granulated sugar placed above or below the brood area for mite control; sometimes includes the antibiotic Terramycin

name: Fanning
definition: The worker bees fan the hive to regulate the temperature, or to evaporate the moisture out of the honey. If they need to cool down the hive, workers stand outside the hive and direct the air in. If they need to dry down the honey, they fan from inside and direct the air out.

name: Feeders
definition: A product used by beekeepers to feed pollen or liquid sugar syrup to honey bees in a colony. There are numerous types of feeders available; front entrance feeders, hive top tank feeders, frame feeders and baggie feeders are a few of the more popular ones.

name: Feral bees
definition: unmanaged colony of bees living in a tree hollow or other enclosed structure

name: Feral Bees; Feral Hive
definition: Bees that are not kept inside of a beehive, otherwise know as a wild hive. This can also refer to a swarm that came out of a "domesticated" hive that now lives as a wild hive.

name: Fermenting Honey
definition: Honey that is going to be extracted should be mostly capped before being extracted. If it isn't, it can contain too much moisture yet. Fermented honey is honey that contains greater than 20% water, in which yeast has grown and caused it to turn into a mixture of carbon dioxide, water and alcohol. The honey will be bubbly and foamy.

name: Fertilized Eggs
definition: Eggs that are laid by the queen that develop into worker bees or queens. Eggs that are unfertilized turn into drone bees only.

name: Festooning
definition: Young bees gorge themselves with honey and hang together by their feet between combs in the hive. Normally this is thought to be a way to measure and build comb in an area that is bigger than normal bee space.

name: Field bees
definition: worker bees at least three weeks old that work (forage) outside the hive to collect nectar, pollen, water, and plant saps for making propolis

name: Field Bees; Worker Bees
definition: Bees from the hive whose job is mainly to fly back and forth from the hive, collecting nectar, pollen and water for the colony.

name: Filtering
definition: The process of removing all foreign parts (such as beeswax, propolis, and other hive matter) from the honey to get it ready for human consumption.

name: Flash heater
definition: a device for heating honey very rapidly to prevent it from being damaged by sustained periods of high temperature

name: Flight Path
definition: The direction bees fly leaving their colony and to return to it. If obstructed, the bees will back up behind whatever object is blocking their path and will become agitated.

name: Flow
definition: The honey flow is a period of availability of nectar, usually more than one source, along with suitable weather for the bees to be able to fly out to gather the nectar. The most obvious sign of a honey flow is the level of activity in and out of the hive, normally starting in the early morning and lasting until mid evening. You can also tell by walking near the hives after dark; you can hear the hum of the bees working on drying down the nectar, and you can smell the sweet honey smell coming out of the hives.

name: Food Chamber
definition: A hive body that has been filled with honey for winter stores.

name: Food Grade Grease
definition: Grease that is not toxic when consumed and it doesn't have toxic additives like lead, lithium and zinc. Food grade grease is used on honey extracting equipment on any part that needs greased that will come in contact with honey.

name: Forage
definition: Forage can be meant as a natural food source for bees (nectar and pollen), such as a "forage area". It can also be meant as what bees do, as in the act of gathering food.

name: Foraging
definition: The term used for what field bees do when they are out of the hive gathering food for the colony.

name: Foulbrood
definition: There are two different types of foulbrood that can infect brood in the bee hive: American and European. They are called foulbrood because they affect the brood, and because these diseases cause the hive to have a certain foul odor. Relatively inexpensive test kits are available to test a hive for foul brood.

name: Foulbrood disease
definition: see “American foulbrood” or “European foulbrood.”

name: Foundation Support Pins
definition: Insert these pins into the holes in the end bars of the frame to help hold beeswax foundation in place.

name: Foundationless
definition: A style of beekeeping in which the bees are given frames with foundation starter strips, and the bees have to draw the comb down on their own from the strips.

name: Frame Feeder
definition: Also known as a division board feeder; a tank that holds sugar water feed for bees and is hung in a hive in place of a frame. These are usually made of plastic for extra durability and ease of cleaning.

name: Frame Grip
